# Compound Agent Active

> **Context Recovery**: Run `npx ca prime` after compaction, clear, or new session

## CLI Commands (ALWAYS USE THESE)

**You MUST use CLI commands for lesson management:**

| Command | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `npx ca search "query"` | Search lessons - MUST call before architectural decisions; use anytime you need context |
| `npx ca learn "insight"` | Capture lessons - call AFTER corrections or discoveries |

## Core Constraints

**Default**: Use CLI commands for lesson management
**Prohibited**: NEVER edit .claude/lessons/ files directly

**Default**: Propose lessons freely after corrections
**Prohibited**: NEVER propose without quality gate (novel + specific; prefer actionable)

## Retrieval Protocol

You MUST call `npx ca search` BEFORE:
- Architectural decisions or complex planning
- Implementing patterns you've done before in this repo

**NEVER skip search for complex decisions.** Past mistakes will repeat.

Beyond mandatory triggers, use these commands freely — they are lightweight queries, not heavyweight operations. Uncertain about a pattern? `ca search`. The cost of an unnecessary search is near-zero; the cost of a missed one can be hours.

## Capture Protocol

Run `npx ca learn` AFTER:
- User corrects you ("no", "wrong", "actually...")
- You self-correct after iteration failures
- Test fails then you fix it

**Quality gate** (must pass before capturing):
- Novel (not already stored)
- Specific (clear guidance)
- Actionable (preferred, not mandatory)

**Workflow**: Search BEFORE deciding, capture AFTER learning.

output:
# Beads Workflow Context

> **Context Recovery**: Run `bd prime` after compaction, clear, or new session
> Hooks auto-call this in Claude Code when .beads/ detected

# 🚨 SESSION CLOSE PROTOCOL 🚨

**CRITICAL**: Before saying "done" or "complete", you MUST run this checklist:

```
[ ] 1. git status              (check what changed)
[ ] 2. git add <files>         (stage code changes)
[ ] 3. git commit -m "..."     (commit code)
[ ] 4. git push                (push to remote)
```

**NEVER skip this.** Work is not done until pushed.

## Core Rules
- **Default**: Use beads for ALL task tracking (`bd create`, `bd ready`, `bd close`)
- **Prohibited**: Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking
- **Workflow**: Create beads issue BEFORE writing code, mark in_progress when starting
- **Memory**: Use `bd remember "insight"` for persistent knowledge across sessions. Do NOT use MEMORY.md files — they fragment across accounts. Search with `bd memories <keyword>`.
- Persistence you don't need beats lost context
- Git workflow: beads auto-commit to Dolt, run `git push` at session end
- Session management: check `bd ready` for available work

## Essential Commands

### Finding Work
- `bd ready` - Show issues ready to work (no blockers)
- `bd list --status=open` - All open issues
- `bd list --status=in_progress` - Your active work
- `bd show <id>` - Detailed issue view with dependencies

### Creating & Updating
- `bd create --title="Summary of this issue" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task|bug|feature --priority=2` - New issue
  - Priority: 0-4 or P0-P4 (0=critical, 2=medium, 4=backlog). NOT "high"/"medium"/"low"
- `bd update <id> --claim` - Claim work
- `bd update <id> --assignee=username` - Assign to someone
- `bd update <id> --title/--description/--notes/--design` - Update fields inline
- `bd close <id>` - Mark complete
- `bd close <id1> <id2> ...` - Close multiple issues at once (more efficient)
- `bd close <id> --reason="explanation"` - Close with reason
- **Tip**: When creating multiple issues/tasks/epics, use parallel subagents for efficiency
- **WARNING**: Do NOT use `bd edit` - it opens $EDITOR (vim/nano) which blocks agents

### Dependencies & Blocking
- `bd dep add <issue> <depends-on>` - Add dependency (issue depends on depends-on)
- `bd blocked` - Show all blocked issues
- `bd show <id>` - See what's blocking/blocked by this issue

### Sync & Collaboration
- `bd dolt push` - Push beads to Dolt remote
- `bd dolt pull` - Pull beads from Dolt remote
- `bd search <query>` - Search issues by keyword

### Project Health
- `bd stats` - Project statistics (open/closed/blocked counts)
- `bd doctor` - Check for issues (sync problems, missing hooks)

## Common Workflows

**Starting work:**
```bash
bd ready           # Find available work
bd show <id>       # Review issue details
bd update <id> --claim  # Claim it
```

**Completing work:**
```bash
bd close <id1> <id2> ...    # Close all completed issues at once
git add . && git commit -m "..."  # Commit code changes
git push                    # Push to remote
```

**Creating dependent work:**
```bash
# Run bd create commands in parallel (use subagents for many items)
bd create --title="Implement feature X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=feature
bd create --title="Write tests for X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task
bd dep add beads-yyy beads-xxx  # Tests depend on Feature (Feature blocks tests)
```

content.content:
○ long-running-harness-nl5 [EPIC] · E11: Piano Roll & MIDI Editor   [● P1 · OPEN]
Owner: Nathan Delacrétaz · Type: epic
Created: 2026-03-28 · Updated: 2026-03-28

DESCRIPTION
Canvas-based note grid editor (pitch vertical x time horizontal, NOT React DOM per NFR-13). Note input: click to place, drag to set duration. Note selection: click, shift-click, rubber-band lasso. Note operations: move (drag), resize (edge handles), delete (Delete key). Velocity editing: bottom lane with per-note bars. Quantization: snap notes to grid at configurable resolution (1/4, 1/8, 1/16, triplets). CC lane editing (mod wheel, expression). Zoom and scroll (independent from arrangement view). Virtual keyboard sidebar for note preview (sounds synth on click). Web MIDI API input (R-OPT-01) with validation layer: clamp note/CC to 0-127, pitch bend to 0-16383, SysEx cap 1KB, all MIDI as untrusted (NFR-16). Live MIDI recording: arm instrument track, press play, record incoming MIDI to new clip. Keyboard shortcuts for common ops (pencil, select, erase tools). EARS: R-EVT-04, R-EVT-17, R-OPT-01, NFR-04, NFR-13, NFR-16. Interface exports: PianoRollEditor, MIDINoteEvent, MIDIInputValidator. QA: note placement precision, quantization correctness, MIDI input validation.

DEPENDS ON
  → ✓ long-running-harness-13h: E9: Synthesizer Engine ● P1
  → ✓ long-running-harness-5he: E2: Neo-Brutalist Design System ● P1
  → ✓ long-running-harness-8v1: E6: Track System & Arrangement View ● P0

BLOCKS
  ← ○ long-running-harness-tnf: (EPIC) E18: Integration Verification ● P1

1	import { z } from "zod/v4";
2	
3	export const clipSchema = z.object({
4	  id: z.string(),
5	  trackId: z.string(),
6	  sourceId: z.string(),
7	  startTime: z.number().min(0),
8	  sourceOffset: z.number().min(0),
9	  duration: z.number().min(0),
10	  gain: z.number().min(0).max(2),
11	  fadeIn: z.number().min(0),
12	  fadeOut: z.number().min(0),
13	  name: z.string(),
14	});
15	
16	export type ClipSchema = z.infer<typeof clipSchema>;
17	
18	export const trackSchema = z.object({
19	  id: z.string(),
20	  name: z.string(),
21	  type: z.enum(["audio", "instrument"]),
22	  color: z.string().regex(/^#[0-9a-fA-F]{6}$/),
23	  muted: z.boolean(),
24	  solo: z.boolean(),
25	  armed: z.boolean(),
26	  soloIsolate: z.boolean(),
27	  volume: z.number().min(0).max(2),
28	  pan: z.number().min(-1).max(1),
29	  clipIds: z.array(z.string()),
30	});
31	
32	export type TrackSchema = z.infer<typeof trackSchema>;

1	/**
2	 * Shared types for the synthesizer engine.
3	 * Used by both the AudioWorklet processor and main thread.
4	 *
5	 * Exports: SynthParameterMap, SynthVoiceCommand (as specified by epic).
6	 */
7	
8	import type { WaveformType } from "./dsp/polyblep";
9	import type { FilterType } from "./dsp/biquad-coeffs";
10	import type { LFOShape } from "./dsp/lfo";
11	
12	// ─── Voice Commands (main thread → worklet) ───
13	
14	export type SynthVoiceCommand =
15	  | { type: "noteOn"; note: number; velocity: number; legato: boolean }
16	  | { type: "noteOff"; note: number }
17	  | { type: "allNotesOff" }
18	  | { type: "setParam"; key: string; value: number };
19	
20	// ─── Parameter Map ───
21	
22	export type SynthParameterMap = {
23	  // Oscillator 1
24	  osc1Type: WaveformType;
25	  osc1Octave: number; // -2..+2
26	  osc1Detune: number; // cents, -100..+100
27	  osc1Level: number; // 0..1
28	
29	  // Oscillator 2
30	  osc2Type: WaveformType;
31	  osc2Octave: number;
32	  osc2Detune: number;
33	  osc2Level: number;
34	
35	  // Filter
36	  filterType: FilterType;
37	  filterCutoff: number; // Hz, 20..20000
38	  filterResonance: number; // Q, 0.5..20
39	
40	  // Amplitude Envelope
41	  ampAttack: number; // seconds
42	  ampDecay: number;
43	  ampSustain: number; // 0..1
44	  ampRelease: number;
45	
46	  // Filter Envelope
47	  filterAttack: number;
48	  filterDecay: number;
49	  filterSustain: number; // 0..1
50	  filterRelease: number;
51	  filterEnvDepth: number; // semitones, -60..+60
52	
53	  // LFO 1
54	  lfo1Rate: number; // Hz, 0.1..20
55	  lfo1Depth: number; // 0..1
56	  lfo1Shape: LFOShape;
57	
58	  // LFO 2
59	  lfo2Rate: number; // Hz, 0.1..20
60	  lfo2Depth: number; // 0..1
61	  lfo2Shape: LFOShape;
62	
63	  // Glide
64	  glideTime: number; // seconds, 0..1
65	
66	  // Master
67	  masterGain: number; // 0..1
68	};
69	
70	/** Default parameter values */
71	export const DEFAULT_SYNTH_PARAMS: SynthParameterMap = {
72	  osc1Type: "saw",
73	  osc1Octave: 0,
74	  osc1Detune: 0,
75	  osc1Level: 0.8,
76	
77	  osc2Type: "saw",
78	  osc2Octave: 0,
79	  osc2Detune: 7, // Slight detune for thickness
80	  osc2Level: 0.5,
81	
82	  filterType: "lowpass",
83	  filterCutoff: 5000,
84	  filterResonance: 1,
85	
86	  ampAttack: 0.01,
87	  ampDecay: 0.2,
88	  ampSustain: 0.7,
89	  ampRelease: 0.3,
90	
91	  filterAttack: 0.01,
92	  filterDecay: 0.3,
93	  filterSustain: 0.3,
94	  filterRelease: 0.5,
95	  filterEnvDepth: 24,
96	
97	  lfo1Rate: 5,
98	  lfo1Depth: 0,
99	  lfo1Shape: "sine",
100	
101	  lfo2Rate: 3,
102	  lfo2Depth: 0,
103	  lfo2Shape: "sine",
104	
105	  glideTime: 0,
106	
107	  masterGain: 0.7,
108	};
109	
110	// ─── MIDI → Frequency Conversion ───
111	
112	/** Convert MIDI note number to frequency in Hz. A4 = 69 = 440Hz. */
113	export function midiToFreq(note: number): number {
114	  return 440 * Math.pow(2, (note - 69) / 12);
115	}
116	
117	/** Waveform type index mapping for AudioParam/message passing */
118	export const WAVEFORM_TYPES: readonly WaveformType[] = [
119	  "sine",
120	  "saw",
121	  "square",
122	  "triangle",
123	];
124	
125	/** Filter type index mapping */
126	export const FILTER_TYPES: readonly FilterType[] = [
127	  "lowpass",
128	  "highpass",
129	  "bandpass",
130	];
131	
132	/** LFO shape index mapping */
133	export const LFO_SHAPES: readonly LFOShape[] = [
134	  "sine",
135	  "square",
136	  "triangle",
137	  "sample-and-hold",
138	];

input.args: Epic: long-running-harness-nl5 - E11: Piano Roll & MIDI Editor. Canvas-based note grid editor (pitch vertical x time horizontal, NOT React DOM per NFR-13). Note input: click to place, drag to set duration. Note selection: click, shift-click, rubber-band lasso. Note operations: move (drag), resize (edge handles), delete (Delete key). Velocity editing: bottom lane with per-note bars. Quantization: snap notes to grid at configurable resolution (1/4, 1/8, 1/16, triplets). CC lane editing (mod wheel, expression). Zoom and scroll (independent from arrangement view). Virtual keyboard sidebar for note preview (sounds synth on click). Web MIDI API input (R-OPT-01) with validation layer: clamp note/CC to 0-127, pitch bend to 0-16383, SysEx cap 1KB, all MIDI as untrusted (NFR-16). Live MIDI recording: arm instrument track, press play, record incoming MIDI to new clip. Keyboard shortcuts for common ops (pencil, select, erase tools). EARS: R-EVT-04, R-EVT-17, R-OPT-01, NFR-04, NFR-13, NFR-16. Interface exports: PianoRollEditor, MIDINoteEvent, MIDIInputValidator. QA: note placement precision, quantization correctness, MIDI input validation.

file.content:
---
name: Plan
description: Decompose work into small testable tasks with clear dependencies
---

# Plan Skill

## Overview
Create a concrete implementation plan by decomposing work into small, testable tasks with dependencies and acceptance criteria.

## Methodology
1. Read the spec from the epic description (`bd show <epic>`) for EARS requirements, decisions, and open questions. Verify its type is `epic` -- if it was created as `task`, fix with `bd update <id> --type=epic`
2. Search memory with `ca search` and docs with `ca knowledge "relevant topic"` for architectural patterns and past mistakes
3. Spawn **subagents** via Task tool in parallel for research (lightweight, no inter-agent coordination):
   - Available agents: `.claude/agents/compound/repo-analyst.md`, `memory-analyst.md`
   - For complex features, deploy MULTIPLE analysts per domain area
   - Synthesize all findings before decomposing into tasks
4. For decisions requiring deep technical grounding, invoke the **researcher skill** to produce a survey document. Review findings before decomposing into tasks.
5. Synthesize research findings into a coherent approach. Flag conflicts between ADRs and proposed plan.
6. Use `AskUserQuestion` to resolve ambiguities, conflicting constraints, or priority trade-offs before decomposing
7. Decompose into tasks small enough to verify individually
8. Define acceptance criteria for each task
9. Ensure each task traces back to a spec requirement for traceability
10. **Generate Acceptance Criteria table**: Extract testable criteria from EARS requirements and append to the epic description. Use this format:

    ```markdown
    ## Acceptance Criteria
    | ID | Source Req | Criterion | Verification Method |
    |----|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
    | AC-1 | EARS-N | When X, system shall Y within Z | unit test / manual / integration |
    ```

    Rules:
    - Each EARS requirement MUST map to at least one AC row
    - Criteria MUST be testable (no vague adjectives like "fast" or "good")
    - Verification method MUST be specified
    - Write the AC table to the epic via `bd update <epic-id> --description="<existing desc + AC section>"`
    - The AC section is **append-only** after plan phase; review annotates pass/fail
11. **Generate Verification Contract**: Derive a minimal, per-epic definition of done and append it to the epic description after the Acceptance Criteria section. Use this format:

    ```markdown
    ## Verification Contract
    Profile: webapp | api | cli | library | service | mixed
    Surfaces:
    - ui_surface
    Risks:
    - user_visible_quality
    Required evidence:
    - test
    - lint
    - build
    ```

    Rules:
    - Detect the profile from repo signals plus the epic's actual scope. If architect/spec work noted a default profile, use it as advisory input only.
    - Keep the contract **local to this epic**. Do NOT create repo-global config for this.
    - Start from a baseline and then tailor:
      - `webapp` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `runtime_startup`, `browser_evidence`
      - `api` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `runtime_startup`, `contract_checks`
      - `cli` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `help_version_check`, `command_transcript`
      - `library` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `examples_run`, `public_api_review`
      - `service` -> `test`, `lint`, `build`, `runtime_startup`, `config_validation`
    - Add contract-specific evidence from touched surfaces and risks:
      - `ui_surface` -> `responsive_check`, `edge_states_check`, `console_network_clean`, `a11y_smoke`, `design_craft_check`
      - `public_api` -> `contract_examples`, `backward_compat_review`
      - `persistence_schema` -> `roundtrip_test`, `migration_check`, `backcompat_check`
      - `packaging_or_distribution` -> `package_build`, `install_smoke`
      - `docs_or_examples` -> `docs_examples_sync`
      - `auth_or_security` -> `auth_failure_paths`
      - `performance_sensitive` -> `performance_budget_check`
    - Use a small, explicit vocabulary for `Surfaces` and `Risks`; prefer consistency over novelty.
    - If the profile is ambiguous **and** the choice materially changes required evidence, resolve it with `AskUserQuestion` once before finalizing the plan.
    - Write the Verification Contract to the epic via `bd update <epic-id> --description="<existing desc + AC section + Verification Contract section>"`
    - The Verification Contract is **append-only** after plan; review may escalate it explicitly if risk was underestimated.
12. Map dependencies between tasks
13. Create beads issues: `bd create --title="..." --type=task`
14. Create review and compound blocking tasks (`bd create` + `bd dep add`) that depend on work tasks — these survive compaction and surface via `bd ready` after work completes

## Memory Integration
- Run `ca search` and `ca knowledge "relevant topic"` for patterns related to the feature area
- Look for past planning mistakes (missing dependencies, unclear criteria)
- Check for preferred architectural patterns in this codebase

## Docs Integration
- Spawn docs-analyst to scan `docs/` for relevant specs, standards, and research
- Check `docs/decisions/` for existing ADRs that constrain or inform the plan
- If the plan contradicts an ADR, flag it for the user before proceeding

## Common Pitfalls
- Creating too many fine-grained tasks (aim for 3-7 per feature)
- Unclear acceptance criteria ("make it work" is not a criterion)
- Missing dependencies between tasks
- Not checking memory for past architectural decisions
- Not reviewing existing ADRs and docs for constraints
- Making architectural decisions without research backing (use the researcher skill for complex domains)
- Planning implementation details too early (stay at task level)
- Not generating Acceptance Criteria table from EARS requirements
- Not generating a Verification Contract, leaving later phases to guess what evidence is required

## Quality Criteria
- Each task has clear acceptance criteria
- Dependencies are mapped and no circular dependencies exist
- Tasks are ordered so each can be verified independently
- Memory was searched for relevant patterns and past mistakes
- Existing docs and ADRs were checked for constraints
- Ambiguities resolved via `AskUserQuestion` before decomposing
- Complexity estimates are realistic (no "should be quick")
- Each task traces back to a spec requirement
- **Acceptance Criteria table generated and appended to epic description**
- **Verification Contract generated and appended to epic description**

## POST-PLAN VERIFICATION -- MANDATORY
After creating all tasks, verify review and compound tasks exist:
- Run `bd list --status=open` and check for a "Review:" task and a "Compound:" task
- If either is missing, CREATE THEM NOW. The plan is NOT complete without these gates.
- **Verify AC table**: Run `bd show <epic-id>` and confirm the `## Acceptance Criteria` section exists in the description. If missing, the plan is NOT complete.
- **Verify contract**: Run `bd show <epic-id>` and confirm the `## Verification Contract` section exists in the description. If missing, the plan is NOT complete.

Thoroughly analyze the existing data model in this DAW project to understand what needs to be extended for MIDI/piano roll:

1. Read src/state/track/types.ts - understand TrackModel, ClipModel, all types
2. Read src/state/store.ts - understand the Zustand store structure, especially clip-related methods

4. Read src/state/undo/ - understand how undo commands work
5. Read the arrangement renderer and interaction hooks - understand the canvas rendering pattern in detail
6. Check if there's any existing MIDI or note event types anywhere in the codebase

I need to understand:
- How ClipModel works and what needs to change for MIDI clips
- How the store manages clips and what methods exist
- The undo command pattern in detail
- The exact canvas rendering and interaction patterns used

1	# ADR-0001: Initial Tooling Choices
2	
3	**Status**: accepted
4	**Date**: 2026-03-25
5	
6	## Context
7	
8	This project needs issue tracking and session memory for AI agent collaboration from the start. Traditional tools (GitHub Issues, wikis) don't integrate well with agent workflows -- they require API calls, context switching, and lack dependency awareness.
9	
10	## Decision
11	
12	- **beads** (`bd`) for issue tracking -- Dolt-powered, dependency-aware, agent-optimized with JSON output and `bd ready` for unblocked work detection.
13	- **compound-agent** (`ca`) for session memory -- captures lessons learned during development, provides semantic search over past decisions and mistakes.
14	
15	Both tools are CLI-first and operate entirely within the repo (no external services required).
16	
17	## Consequences
18	
19	- Agents can discover, claim, and complete work without leaving the terminal.
20	- Lessons persist across sessions, reducing repeated mistakes.
21	- Requires `bd` and `ca` CLI tools to be installed for contributors.
22	- Issue data lives in `.beads/` (Dolt), lessons in `.claude/lessons/` (JSONL + SQLite).
23

1	# Advisory Fleet Brief: BRUTALWAV
2	
3	**Advisors consulted**: Security & Reliability (Claude Sonnet), Scalability & Performance (Gemini), Simplicity & Alternatives (Claude Opus)
4	**Advisors unavailable**: Organizational & Delivery (Codex -- timed out during execution)
5	
6	---
7	
8	## P0 Concerns
9	
10	### 1. SharedArrayBuffer requires cross-origin isolation headers (Security)
11	The lock-free audio/UI communication via SharedArrayBuffer silently fails without `Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin` + `Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp`. The spec treats SAB as a given but this is a hard deployment constraint.
12	- **Action**: Promote to NFR. Add startup check: `crossOriginIsolated === true` or hard error. Document required server headers.
13	
14	### 2. Memory exhaustion via fully-decoded AudioBuffers (Scalability)
15	A 5-minute stereo track at 44.1kHz/32-bit = ~105MB. 50 tracks = ~5.2GB. Browser tabs will crash.
16	- **Action**: Implement chunked streaming from IndexedDB/File System Access API. Use AudioBuffer only for short samples. Consider MediaElementAudioSourceNode for long backing tracks.
17	
18	### 3. React re-render thrashing for 60fps metering (Scalability)
19	Feeding 60fps metering through Zustand/React state will cause massive render tree updates.
20	- **Action**: Bypass React state for high-frequency updates. Use requestAnimationFrame to read directly from SharedArrayBuffer and mutate raw DOM/canvas via useRef.
21	
22	### 4. Feedback loop detection has no implementation contract (Security)
23	R-UNW-05 names the requirement but gives no implementation. Cycles cause runaway gain -- speaker/hearing damage.
24	- **Action**: Mandatory DFS cycle detection on every routing mutation. Reject the command before AudioNode connection is made.
25	
26	### 5. Full analog-modeled 808 from scratch is the biggest complexity risk (Simplicity)
27	11 drum instruments, each a mini-synthesizer with AudioWorklet. 3-4x effort of sample-based drums with marginal perceptible benefit.
28	- **Action**: Ship with high-quality 808 samples + Tone/Decay/Tune knobs for tweakability. Make analog modeling a future epic.
29	
30	### 6. "AudioWorklet for ALL DSP" is an over-constraint (Simplicity)
31	Native nodes (BiquadFilterNode, DynamicsCompressorNode, ConvolverNode, etc.) are C++ implementations -- faster and more battle-tested than JS AudioWorklet equivalents.
32	- **Action**: Relax R-UBI-02. Use native Web Audio nodes where they suffice. Reserve AudioWorklet for custom DSP only (synth voices, custom envelopes, modulation).
33	
34	---
35	
36	## P1 Concerns
37	
38	### 7. AudioWorklet GC pauses will cause audio dropouts (Scalability)
39	Memory allocation in the process() loop triggers GC, causing immediate audio artifacts.
40	- **Action**: Mandate zero-allocation coding in AudioWorklet process(). Pre-allocate all buffers during initialization. Consider WASM for heavy DSP.
41	
42	### 8. Decoded audio buffer size is unbounded (Security)
43	A 192kHz/32-bit/8-channel 60-minute FLAC = ~25GB decoded. DoS vector.
44	- **Action**: File size limit + estimated decoded size check before decodeAudioData. Reject oversized buffers.
45	
46	### 9. Session JSON has no schema validation (Security)
47	JSON.parse of arbitrary input risks prototype pollution. Crafted sessions could produce invalid DSP topology.
48	- **Action**: Validate with Zod schema. Include schemaVersion. Recovery = load what validates, discard the rest.
49	
50	### 10. OfflineAudioContext bounce is unbounded in memory (Security)
51	10-minute stereo at 48kHz = ~230MB for the raw buffer alone.
52	- **Action**: Chunked render strategy (30-second blocks), stream to FileSystemWritableFileStream.
53	
54	### 11. MIDI input has no validation boundary (Security)
55	SysEx floods, out-of-range CC values passed to AudioParam could produce NaN/Infinity.
56	- **Action**: MIDI input validation layer: clamp all values, enforce max SysEx length, treat all MIDI as untrusted.
57	
58	### 12. Modulation matrix is premature for v1 (Simplicity)
59	Full drag-to-connect routing is a major UI/DSP subsystem. Popular synths ship without it.
60	- **Action**: Hardwire standard modulation paths (filter env -> cutoff, LFO -> pitch/cutoff/amp, velocity -> filter/amp). Matrix deferred to later.
61	
62	### 13. Full mixer routing (sidechain, sends, buses) on day one is scope creep (Simplicity)
63	Requires bus architecture, cycle detection, topological sort updates on every change.
64	- **Action**: v1 ships insert-only effects + master bus. Add sends/buses/sidechain as later epic.
65	
66	### 14. Custom design system from scratch is high-effort, low-leverage (Simplicity)
67	Building every UI primitive (buttons, sliders, dropdowns, modals) from scratch absorbs 20-30% of UI time.
68	- **Action**: Start with shadcn/ui (Radix primitives) + brutalist theme. Custom-build only DAW-specific controls (knobs, faders, meters, piano roll).
69	
70	### 15. 50-track session load < 2s is unrealistic without lazy loading (Scalability)
71	decodeAudioData is CPU-bound. 50 files takes >>2 seconds.
72	- **Action**: Lazy load. Persist pre-computed waveform peaks in IndexedDB. Render UI instantly, decode audio in background.
73	
74	### 16. AudioWorklet quantum overrun has no degradation contract (Security)
75	Spec says "log and continue" but doesn't define what "continue" means.
76	- **Action**: Define: emit silence for that quantum, post overrun message, mute track after N consecutive overruns.
77	
78	---
79	
80	## P2 Concerns
81	
82	### 17. DOM overload in piano roll and arrangement (Scalability)
83	Thousands of MIDI notes and waveform SVGs will overwhelm the layout engine.
84	- **Action**: Use HTML5 Canvas or WebGL for timeline, waveforms, and piano roll.
85	
86	### 18. Look-ahead scheduler drift from main-thread blocking (Scalability)
87	setInterval on UI thread is vulnerable to React rendering blocks.
88	- **Action**: Move scheduler to a Web Worker or manage timing inside AudioWorklet.
89	
90	### 19. Both convolution AND algorithmic reverb is redundant (Simplicity)
91	Two implementations, double the testing.
92	- **Action**: Ship ConvolverNode reverb only for v1. Add algorithmic later.
93	
94	### 20. Arpeggiator is nice-to-have, not core (Simplicity)
95	Self-contained feature that doesn't block any workflow.
96	- **Action**: Defer. Piano roll covers manual arpeggio patterns.
97	
98	### 21. IndexedDB writes aren't transactionally safe (Security)
99	Browser crash mid-write = corrupt session.
100	- **Action**: Write-then-swap pattern with session_draft -> session_current + session_backup.
101	
102	### 22. Undo stack holds unbounded buffer references (Security)
103	Long sessions accumulate hundreds of commands holding AudioBuffer references.
104	- **Action**: Max undo depth (200), buffer references as IDs into separate cache with eviction.
105	
106	### 23. Dual-state pattern over-applied (Simplicity)
107	Not all state needs lock-free communication. Session metadata is UI-only.
108	- **Action**: Dual-state only for audio-thread-relevant state (transport, params, graph topology). Keep rest in Zustand.
109	
110	### 24. File import lacks magic-bytes validation (Security)
111	Extension-based validation is trivially bypassed.
112	- **Action**: Check first 12 bytes for WAV/MP3/OGG/FLAC signatures before decodeAudioData.
113	
114	---
115	
116	## Strengths (Consensus)
117	
118	All three advisors agreed on these architectural strengths:
119	
120	1. **AudioWorklet thread isolation** is the correct architecture for browser DSP (all three)
121	2. **Look-ahead scheduler pattern** is the only way to get sample-accurate timing (Scalability, Simplicity)
122	3. **Non-destructive editing** with clip references eliminates corruption bugs (Security, Scalability)
123	4. **React 19 + Zustand** is appropriately simple -- Tone.js abstraction would fight custom DSP (Simplicity)
124	5. **OfflineAudioContext for bounce/export** is the standard, correct approach (Scalability, Simplicity)
125	6. **IndexedDB persistence** without server complexity is pragmatic (Simplicity)
126	7. **Command-pattern undo/redo** is the right architecture for heterogeneous edit operations (Security, Simplicity)
127	8. **Desktop-only (1024px min)** is wise -- DAWs are desktop tools (Simplicity)
128	9. **Strict TypeScript + 32-bit float** reduces coercion bugs at AudioParam boundaries (Security)
129	10. **16-voice polyphony ceiling** matches browser capability (Simplicity)
130	
131	---
132	
133	## Alternative Approaches
134	
135	**From Simplicity advisor (Claude Opus, HIGH confidence):**
136	
137	The "simplest viable DAW" that still delivers a real product:
138	- Sample-based 808 with tweakable parameters (not analog-modeled synthesis)
139	- Native Web Audio nodes for standard effects
140	- Insert-only effects chain (no sends/buses/sidechain v1)
141	- Hardwired synth modulation paths (not full matrix)
142	- shadcn/ui with brutalist theme (not from-scratch design system)
143	- Convolution reverb only (not dual implementations)
144	- No arpeggiator
145	
146	This still delivers: synthesizer with PolyBLEP oscillators + filters + ADSR + LFOs, 808 patterns with tweakable samples, effects, mixer with faders/inserts, piano roll, arrangement, save/load, bounce.
147	
148	---
149	
150	## Confidence Summary
151	
152	| Advisor | Confidence | Justification |
153	|---------|-----------|---------------|
154	| Security & Reliability | MEDIUM | Right requirements named, but cross-origin isolation absent, degradation contracts underspecified, trust model implicit |
155	| Scalability & Performance | HIGH | Browser DAW limitations well-documented; concerns directly address physical limits of memory, GC, and rendering |
156	| Organizational & Delivery | N/A | Codex timed out |
157	| Simplicity & Alternatives | HIGH | Spec is well-researched but bundles v1 and v3 features; core architecture is sound, risk is in scope size |

1	# BRUTALWAV: Browser-Based Digital Audio Workstation
2	
3	## System Overview
4	
5	BRUTALWAV is a professional browser-based Digital Audio Workstation built on the Web Audio API. It provides multi-track audio and MIDI arrangement, a full-featured synthesizer, TR-808 drum machine (sample-based with tweakable parameters), effects processing, full mixer routing, modulation matrix, arpeggiator, and a piano roll editor -- all within a Neo-Brutalist interface that exposes its structural mechanics.
6	
7	**Target users**: Musicians, producers, and sound designers seeking a capable, browser-native production environment.
8	
9	**Tech stack**: React 19 + TypeScript (strict), Vite, Zustand (UI state), Web Audio API with native nodes for standard effects + AudioWorklet for custom DSP. Canvas/direct DOM for high-frequency rendering (metering, waveforms, piano roll).
10	
11	**Design direction**: Neo-Brutalist Expressionism with bold color accents. JetBrains Mono for data/values, system neo-grotesque for headings. 8px spatial grid. High-contrast black/white base with electric blue, hot pink, acid green, amber, and red accent palette.
12	
13	**Advisory profile**: `webapp` (data-dense, state-heavy, real-time interactive)
14	
15	**Build-great-things note**: All user-facing epics should invoke `/compound:build-great-things` during their work phase. This DAW requires excellence across all six build phases: Foundation (IA, state architecture), Structure (grid, responsive, component architecture), Craft (typography, color system, visual hierarchy), Motion & Interaction (micro-interactions, drag-and-drop, keyboard shortcuts), Performance & Polish (loading states, edge states, accessibility), and Launch (onboarding).
16	
17	---
18	
19	## EARS Requirements
20	
21	### R-UBI: Ubiquitous Requirements (always true)
22	
23	| ID | Requirement |
24	|----|-------------|
25	| R-UBI-01 | The system shall render all audio through the Web Audio API AudioContext |
26	| R-UBI-02 | The system shall use native Web Audio nodes (BiquadFilterNode, DynamicsCompressorNode, ConvolverNode, GainNode, etc.) for standard effects processing, and AudioWorklet processors for custom DSP (synth voices, custom envelopes, modulation routing). No ScriptProcessorNode. |
27	| R-UBI-03 | The system shall maintain separate audio-thread state and UI-thread state, communicating via lock-free mechanisms (MessagePort, SharedArrayBuffer) |
28	| R-UBI-04 | The system shall use sample-accurate scheduling via AudioContext.currentTime for all timed audio events |
29	| R-UBI-05 | The system shall preserve non-destructive editing: clips reference source audio without modifying originals |
30	| R-UBI-06 | The system shall apply the 8px spatial grid for all UI layout |
31	| R-UBI-07 | The system shall support undo/redo for all user-initiated edit operations via command pattern |
32	| R-UBI-08 | The system shall display real-time audio metering (peak + RMS) for all active tracks and master |
33	| R-UBI-09 | The system shall follow the Neo-Brutalist design system: JetBrains Mono for data, high-contrast palette, visible structural borders, exposed grid |
34	| R-UBI-10 | The system shall enforce TypeScript strict mode with noUncheckedIndexedAccess |
35	| R-UBI-11 | The system shall maintain all editable session state as a serializable JSON structure |
36	
37	### R-EVT: Event-Driven Requirements
38	
39	| ID | Trigger | Requirement |
40	|----|---------|-------------|
41	| R-EVT-01 | When the user presses Play | The system shall begin playback from the current cursor position, scheduling audio events using the look-ahead scheduler pattern |
42	| R-EVT-02 | When the user presses Stop | The system shall stop all audio playback, silence all outputs, and return the cursor to the last play-start position |
43	| R-EVT-03 | When the user presses Pause | The system shall suspend playback at the current position, retaining cursor position for resume |
44	| R-EVT-04 | When the user triggers a MIDI note (keyboard/mouse on piano roll or virtual keyboard) | The system shall allocate a synth voice, apply the current patch parameters (oscillators, filter, envelopes, LFOs), and route through the instrument track's signal chain within 10ms |
45	| R-EVT-05 | When the user imports an audio file (WAV/MP3/OGG/FLAC) | The system shall decode the file via AudioContext.decodeAudioData, store it in the media pool, and create a clip on the selected track |
46	| R-EVT-06 | When the user creates a new track | The system shall add a channel strip to the mixer (fader, pan, mute, solo, inserts, sends) and a lane in the arrangement view |
47	| R-EVT-07 | When the user adds an effect to a track insert slot | The system shall instantiate the corresponding AudioNode(s) and insert them into the track's audio graph chain |
48	| R-EVT-08 | When the user changes a synth parameter (oscillator type, filter cutoff, envelope, LFO rate, etc.) | The system shall apply the change in real-time via AudioParam scheduling with no audible glitch |
49	| R-EVT-09 | When the user clicks on the arrangement timeline | The system shall move the playback cursor to the clicked position, snapped to the current grid resolution |
50	| R-EVT-10 | When the user drags a clip on the arrangement | The system shall move the clip non-destructively, updating its timeline position and displaying ghost preview during drag |
51	| R-EVT-11 | When the user splits a clip | The system shall create two clips from the original, each referencing the same source audio with adjusted offsets |
52	| R-EVT-12 | When the user triggers a step on the 808 sequencer | The system shall play the corresponding drum sample (bass drum, snare, hi-hat, etc.) with user-adjustable parameters (tone, decay, tune, volume) via playback rate, amplitude envelope, and filter manipulation |
53	| R-EVT-13 | When the user saves the session | The system shall serialize the full session state to JSON and persist it to browser storage (IndexedDB) |
54	| R-EVT-14 | When the user initiates bounce/export | The system shall render the session to an audio buffer via OfflineAudioContext and offer download as WAV |
55	| R-EVT-15 | When the user activates the arpeggiator | The system shall auto-play held notes in the selected pattern (up/down/up-down/random) at the tempo-synced rate |
56	| R-EVT-16 | When the user adjusts tempo (BPM) | The system shall update the transport clock and reschedule all tempo-dependent events (metronome, arpeggiator, synced LFOs, step sequencer) |
57	| R-EVT-17 | When the user presses a keyboard shortcut | The system shall execute the mapped command without requiring mouse interaction |
58	
59	### R-STA: State-Driven Requirements
60	
61	| ID | State | Requirement |
62	|----|-------|-------------|
63	| R-STA-01 | While the transport is playing | The system shall advance the playback cursor, schedule audio events via the look-ahead scheduler, and update metering at 60fps |
64	| R-STA-02 | While a track is armed for recording | The system shall display the track in recording-ready state (hot pink accent) and monitor audio input if applicable |
65	| R-STA-03 | While loop mode is active | The system shall return the playback cursor to the loop start point when it reaches the loop end point, with seamless crossfade |
66	| R-STA-04 | While a track is soloed | The system shall mute all non-soloed tracks (solo-in-place behavior), except tracks with solo-isolate enabled |
67	| R-STA-05 | While the synthesizer is in legato mode | The system shall not re-trigger envelopes for overlapping notes, instead gliding pitch between notes |
68	| R-STA-06 | While the 808 sequencer is running | The system shall advance through the 16-step pattern at the current tempo, triggering drum sounds with accent and flam as programmed |
69	| R-STA-07 | While the arpeggiator latch is on | The system shall continue playing the arpeggio pattern even after all keys are released |
70	| R-STA-08 | While AudioContext is suspended (no user gesture yet) | The system shall display a clear prompt to click/interact to start audio, and resume the context on the first user gesture |
71	| R-STA-09 | While any parameter automation is active | The system shall apply automated parameter changes at sample-accurate timing, overriding manual knob positions |
72	
73	### R-UNW: Unwanted Behavior Requirements
74	
75	| ID | Condition | Requirement |
76	|----|-----------|-------------|
77	| R-UNW-01 | If audio output clips (exceeds 0dBFS) | The system shall activate the clip indicator on the affected channel and master, persisting until manually cleared |
78	| R-UNW-02 | If AudioWorklet processing exceeds the quantum budget (128 samples) | The system shall log the overrun event and continue processing without crashing |
79	| R-UNW-03 | If audio file decode fails | The system shall display an error with the file name and format, suggesting supported alternatives |
80	| R-UNW-04 | If browser tab loses focus during playback | The system shall continue audio playback uninterrupted (no throttling of audio thread) |
81	| R-UNW-05 | If the user attempts to create a feedback loop in routing | The system shall perform DFS cycle detection on the signal graph before applying any routing mutation, reject the command, and display a warning. This is a mandatory pre-condition for every addRoute, addSend, and addInsert operation. |
82	| R-UNW-06 | If IndexedDB storage is full during session save | The system shall notify the user and offer to export the session as a downloadable file |
83	| R-UNW-07 | If a synthesizer voice exceeds polyphony limit | The system shall steal the oldest or lowest-priority voice using the configured voice allocation strategy |
84	| R-UNW-08 | If the session JSON is malformed on load | The system shall display a recovery dialog and attempt to load as much of the session as possible |
85	
86	### R-OPT: Optional/Feature Requirements
87	
88	| ID | Feature | Requirement |
89	|----|---------|-------------|
90	| R-OPT-01 | Where MIDI input device is connected | The system shall accept MIDI note and CC messages, routing them to the selected instrument track |
91	| R-OPT-02 | Where the user enables sidechain compression | The system shall route the sidechain source signal to the compressor's detector input on the target track |
92	| R-OPT-03 | Where the user activates dark mode / light mode toggle | The system shall switch the full UI to the alternate Neo-Brutalist theme variant |
93	| R-OPT-04 | Where the user opens the modulation matrix | The system shall display all available modulation sources and destinations with drag-to-connect routing |
94	| R-OPT-05 | Where the user loads a preset | The system shall deserialize the preset JSON and apply all synthesizer/effect parameters atomically |
95	
96	---
97	
98	## Architecture Diagrams
99	
100	### C4 Context Diagram
101	
102	```mermaid
103	C4Context
104	    title BRUTALWAV System Context
105	
106	    Person(musician, "Musician / Producer", "Creates music using the DAW")
107	
108	    System(brutalwav, "BRUTALWAV", "Browser-based DAW with synth, 808, mixer, effects, piano roll, arrangement")
109	
110	    System_Ext(webaudio, "Web Audio API", "Browser audio engine: AudioContext, AudioWorklet, AudioNodes")
111	    System_Ext(indexeddb, "IndexedDB", "Browser persistent storage for sessions and audio files")
112	    System_Ext(midi, "Web MIDI API", "External MIDI controller input")
113	    System_Ext(filesystem, "File System Access API", "Import/export audio files and sessions")
114	
115	    Rel(musician, brutalwav, "Composes, arranges, mixes, exports")
116	    Rel(brutalwav, webaudio, "Creates audio graph, schedules events, renders audio")
117	    Rel(brutalwav, indexeddb, "Persists sessions, presets, media pool")
118	    Rel(brutalwav, midi, "Receives MIDI note/CC input")
119	    Rel(brutalwav, filesystem, "Imports audio, exports WAV/project")
120	```
121	
122	### C4 Container Diagram
123	
124	```mermaid
125	C4Context
126	    title BRUTALWAV Container Architecture
127	
128	    Container_Boundary(browser, "Browser") {
129	        Container(ui, "UI Layer", "React 19 + TypeScript", "Arrangement, mixer, piano roll, synth editor, 808 pads")
130	        Container(state, "State Manager", "Zustand", "UI state, session model, undo history")
131	        Container(engine, "Audio Engine", "TypeScript + AudioWorklet", "Audio graph, scheduling, DSP processing")
132	        Container(transport, "Transport Controller", "TypeScript", "Play/pause/stop/loop, tempo map, cursor sync")
133	        Container(synth, "Synthesizer Core", "AudioWorklet", "Oscillators, filters, envelopes, LFOs, modulation matrix")
134	        Container(drums, "808 Drum Machine", "AudioWorklet + WebAudio nodes", "Analog-modeled drum synthesis + step sequencer")
135	        Container(effects, "Effects Rack", "WebAudio nodes + AudioWorklet", "Reverb, delay, compressor, EQ, distortion, chorus")
136	        Container(mixer, "Mixer Engine", "TypeScript + WebAudio", "Channel strips, routing, faders, pan, sends, master bus")
137	        Container(media, "Media Pool", "TypeScript + IndexedDB", "Audio file storage, decode, reference management")
138	    }
139	
140	    Rel(ui, state, "Reads/dispatches")
141	    Rel(state, engine, "Commands via MessagePort")
142	    Rel(engine, transport, "Clock sync")
143	    Rel(engine, synth, "Note events")
144	    Rel(engine, drums, "Trigger events")
145	    Rel(engine, effects, "Signal routing")
146	    Rel(engine, mixer, "Channel routing")
147	    Rel(ui, media, "Import/browse files")
148	```
149	
150	### Transport State Diagram
151	
152	```mermaid
153	stateDiagram-v2
154	    [*] --> Stopped
155	    Stopped --> Playing : play()
156	    Stopped --> Stopped : stop() [already stopped]
157	    Playing --> Paused : pause()
158	    Playing --> Stopped : stop()
159	    Playing --> Playing : seek() [reposition cursor]
160	    Paused --> Playing : play() [resume from position]
161	    Paused --> Stopped : stop()
162	    Paused --> Paused : seek()
163	
164	    state Playing {
165	        [*] --> NormalPlay
166	        NormalPlay --> LoopPlay : enableLoop()
167	        LoopPlay --> NormalPlay : disableLoop()
168	        LoopPlay --> LoopPlay : reachLoopEnd [jump to loop start]
169	    }
170	```
171	
172	### Synthesizer Voice Lifecycle
173	
174	```mermaid
175	stateDiagram-v2
176	    [*] --> Idle
177	    Idle --> Attack : noteOn(pitch, velocity)
178	    Attack --> Decay : attackComplete
179	    Decay --> Sustain : decayComplete
180	    Sustain --> Release : noteOff()
181	    Release --> Idle : releaseComplete [voice freed]
182	
183	    Attack --> Release : noteOff() [short note]
184	    Decay --> Release : noteOff() [medium note]
185	
186	    Idle --> Stolen : voiceSteal [polyphony limit]
187	    Stolen --> Attack : noteOn(newPitch) [reassigned]
188	```
189	
190	### Audio Signal Flow Sequence
191	
192	```mermaid
193	sequenceDiagram
194	    participant User
195	    participant UI
196	    participant Transport
197	    participant Scheduler
198	    participant AudioGraph
199	    participant Mixer
200	    participant Master
201	    participant Speakers
202	
203	    User->>UI: Press Play
204	    UI->>Transport: play()
205	    Transport->>Scheduler: start look-ahead loop
206	    loop Every 25ms (look-ahead)
207	        Scheduler->>Scheduler: Check events in next 100ms window
208	        Scheduler->>AudioGraph: Schedule source.start(time), noteOn(time)
209	    end
210	    AudioGraph->>Mixer: Per-track audio signal
211	    Mixer->>Mixer: Apply inserts, fader, pan, sends
212	    Mixer->>Master: Sum to master bus
213	    Master->>Speakers: AudioContext.destination
214	    Transport->>UI: Cursor position update (rAF)
215	```
216	
217	### 808 Step Sequencer Flow
218	
219	```mermaid
220	sequenceDiagram
221	    participant Sequencer
222	    participant Transport
223	    participant Scheduler
224	    participant DrumVoice
225	    participant Mixer
226	
227	    Transport->>Sequencer: Beat clock tick
228	    Sequencer->>Sequencer: Advance to next step (0-15)
229	    Sequencer->>Scheduler: Read pattern: which drums active on this step
230	    loop For each active drum on step
231	        Scheduler->>DrumVoice: triggerDrum(type, accent, time)
232	        DrumVoice->>DrumVoice: Synthesize (oscillator + noise + filter + envelope)
233	        DrumVoice->>Mixer: Audio output to drum bus
234	    end
235	```

238	
239	## Scenario Table
240	
241	| # | Scenario | EARS Refs | Trigger | Expected Behavior | Verification |
242	|---|----------|-----------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
243	| S01 | Basic playback | R-EVT-01, R-STA-01 | User presses Space (Play) | Audio plays from cursor, cursor advances, meters move | Audio output matches expected waveform via OfflineAudioContext |
244	| S02 | Stop and return | R-EVT-02 | User presses Space (Stop) while playing | Audio stops, cursor returns to start position | Transport state = Stopped, cursor = last start |
245	| S03 | Loop playback | R-STA-03 | Enable loop region, press Play | Seamless loop at boundaries | Rendered audio shows seamless transition at loop point |
246	| S04 | Synth note trigger | R-EVT-04 | Press key on virtual keyboard | Note sounds within 10ms, ADSR envelope shapes amplitude | FFT confirms correct pitch, amplitude envelope matches ADSR |
247	| S05 | 808 bass drum | R-EVT-12 | Trigger bass drum step | Characteristic 808 kick: sub-frequency punch with pitch sweep | Spectral analysis shows fundamental < 80Hz with descending pitch envelope |
248	| S06 | 808 pattern play | R-STA-06 | Activate 808 sequencer | 16-step pattern advances at tempo | Drum hits land on expected beat positions (sample-accurate) |
249	| S07 | Import audio | R-EVT-05 | Drag WAV file into arrangement | File decodes, clip appears on track | Decoded buffer matches source file, clip visible in UI |
250	| S08 | Add effect | R-EVT-07 | Add reverb to track insert | Audio passes through reverb processing | Wet/dry comparison shows reverb tail in output |
251	| S09 | Filter sweep | R-EVT-08 | Turn filter cutoff knob on synth | Real-time frequency change, no glitch | Spectrogram shows smooth frequency transition |
252	| S10 | Clip split | R-EVT-11 | Split clip at cursor | Two clips, same source, adjusted offsets | Both clips play identical content to original at their positions |
253	| S11 | Solo track | R-STA-04 | Solo track 2 | Only track 2 audible | Master output = track 2 output (within float precision) |
254	| S12 | Arpeggiator | R-EVT-15, R-STA-07 | Enable arp, hold chord | Notes play in pattern at tempo rate | Note timing matches expected pattern at BPM |
255	| S13 | Polyphony limit | R-UNW-07 | Play more notes than max polyphony | Oldest voice stolen, no silence gap | Continuous audio output, stolen voice fades |
256	| S14 | Clip indicator | R-UNW-01 | Push fader above 0dB with hot signal | Clip LED lights, stays lit | Peak meter shows clip, indicator persists |
257	| S15 | AudioContext resume | R-STA-08 | Open app fresh (no gesture) | Shows "Click to start audio" | AudioContext state = suspended until click |
258	| S16 | Feedback prevention | R-UNW-05 | Route track send to itself | Connection rejected, warning shown | Audio graph has no cycles |
259	| S17 | Session save/load | R-EVT-13, R-UNW-08 | Save session, reload page, load | Session restores identically | All tracks, clips, parameters match saved state |
260	| S18 | Bounce to WAV | R-EVT-14 | Select bounce, choose range | WAV file offered for download | Downloaded WAV matches real-time playback |
261	| S19 | Tempo change | R-EVT-16 | Change BPM from 120 to 140 | All tempo-synced elements update | Metronome, arp, LFO rates recalculate correctly |
262	| S20 | MIDI input | R-OPT-01 | Play note on MIDI controller | Note triggers on selected instrument | Same as S04, triggered via MIDI instead of mouse |
263	| S21 | Sidechain duck | R-OPT-02 | Setup sidechain: kick -> bass compressor | Bass ducks on kick hits | Amplitude analysis shows bass reduction coinciding with kick |
264	| S22 | Undo/redo | R-UBI-07 | Delete clip, then Ctrl+Z | Clip restored | Session state matches pre-delete state |
265	| S23 | Keyboard shortcuts | R-EVT-17 | Press 'M' on track | Track mutes | Track output = silence |
266	
267	---
268	
269	## Non-Functional Requirements
270	
271	| ID | Category | Requirement |
272	|----|----------|-------------|
273	| NFR-01 | Latency | Audio output latency shall be < 20ms from event trigger to audible output |
274	| NFR-02 | Performance | The system shall sustain 16 simultaneous tracks with effects at 60fps UI update rate |
275	| NFR-03 | Performance | Synthesizer shall support 16-voice polyphony without audio glitches |
276	| NFR-04 | Accessibility | All interactive controls shall be keyboard-navigable (WCAG 2.2 AA) |
277	| NFR-05 | Accessibility | prefers-reduced-motion shall be respected for all UI animations |
278	| NFR-06 | Browser Support | Chrome 90+, Firefox 90+, Safari 16+ (AudioWorklet baseline) |
279	| NFR-07 | Responsiveness | Minimum viewport: 1024px width (desktop-focused tool) |
280	| NFR-08 | Storage | Sessions up to 50 tracks shall save/load within 2 seconds |
281	| NFR-09 | Audio Quality | All DSP processing shall use 32-bit float at the AudioContext's native sample rate |
282	| NFR-10 | Testability | All DSP algorithms shall be testable via OfflineAudioContext (deterministic rendering) |
283	| NFR-11 | Deployment | The application server shall set Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin and Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp headers. App shall check crossOriginIsolated === true at startup and display hard error if false. |
284	| NFR-12 | Memory | Audio files shall use chunked streaming from IndexedDB/File System Access API. AudioBuffer used only for short samples (<30s). Long tracks use streaming or MediaElementAudioSourceNode. Imported file size limit: 500MB. |
285	| NFR-13 | Rendering | High-frequency UI updates (metering, waveforms, playback cursor, piano roll) shall bypass React state and render directly to HTML5 Canvas or mutate DOM via useRef, reading from SharedArrayBuffer via requestAnimationFrame. |
286	| NFR-14 | Audio Safety | AudioWorklet process() methods shall follow zero-allocation coding: all buffers pre-allocated at initialization, no object/array creation in the render loop. |
287	| NFR-15 | Data Integrity | Session JSON shall be validated against a Zod schema on load. Schema includes version field. Recovery loads valid portions and discards invalid data. Write uses draft-then-swap pattern with backup. |
288	| NFR-16 | Input Validation | All MIDI input values shall be clamped to valid ranges (note/CC: 0-127, pitch bend: 0-16383). SysEx messages capped at 1KB. All external input treated as untrusted. |
289	| NFR-17 | Export | Bounce/export shall use chunked OfflineAudioContext rendering (30-second blocks) with incremental WAV encoding to avoid single large buffer allocation. |
290

2:title: Advanced CSS and WebGL Visual Craft for Production Websites
4:summary: Documents the modern browser-native toolkit for visually distinctive production web interfaces—CSS blend modes, clip-paths, conic gradients, SVG filter pipelines, scroll-linked animations, backdrop filtering, and WebGL shaders—distinguishing baseline interoperable features from experimental ones. Characterizes trade-offs in performance, accessibility, implementation complexity, and ecosystem maturity across approach families.
8:# Advanced CSS and WebGL Visual Craft for Production Websites
14:This survey documents the modern browser‑native toolkit for constructing visually distinctive but production‑ready web interfaces, focusing on compositing, masking, gradients, filter effects, scroll‑driven motion, backdrop composition, shader‑based backgrounds, and variable font animation in 2025–2026. It emphasizes technologies that ship in evergreen browsers, including CSS blend modes, clip‑paths, CSS masking, conic gradients, SVG filter pipelines, scroll‑linked animations, and backdrop filtering, while situating WebGL and variable fonts as complementary layers for higher‑end experiences. [developer.mozilla](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Reference/Properties/scroll-timeline)
16:The analysis distinguishes between features that are baseline interoperable and those whose support remains partial, experimental, or subject to vendor quirks, using MDN, Can I Use, CSS Snapshot 2025, and browser‑specific documentation as primary evidence. It further characterizes implementation strategies observed in technical articles, performance studies, and compatibility dashboards, including progressive enhancement via feature queries, scroll‑timeline fallbacks, and the layering of CSS, SVG, and WebGL for controllable complexity. No prescriptive ranking is offered; instead, the survey surfaces trade‑offs in performance, accessibility, implementation complexity, and ecosystem maturity across the available approach families. [css-tricks](https://css-tricks.com/bringing-back-parallax-with-scroll-driven-css-animations/)
20:The central question addressed is which browser‑level visual technologies constitute a practical toolkit for distinctive website aesthetics in 2025–2026, and how their capabilities, maturity, and trade‑offs compare. This question matters because the modern platform increasingly exposes low‑level compositing, animation, and typography primitives that historically required custom JavaScript or canvas pipelines, thereby reshaping how designers achieve “premium” visual treatments without abandoning accessibility or performance constraints. [w3](https://www.w3.org/TR/css-2025/)
22:The scope is restricted to platform features that are directly exposed through CSS, SVG, or WebGL, including CSS blend modes, clipping and masking, gradient families, SVG filter effects, noise and grain techniques, scroll‑linked animation primitives, backdrop filtering, shader‑driven backgrounds, and variable font animations. General layout systems (for example, Grid or Flexbox), high‑level JavaScript animation frameworks, and purely graphical assets (for example, pre‑rendered video backgrounds) are excluded, except where they interact with the listed primitives in implementation patterns. [arxiv](https://arxiv.org/pdf/0801.2618.pdf)
28:Understanding this toolkit requires a basic model of the browser rendering pipeline, which transforms markup and style into rasterized pixels through layout, painting, and compositing stages. In this pipeline, properties such as blend modes, clipping, masking, filters, and backdrop filters are typically resolved at the painting or compositing stage, sometimes on GPU‑accelerated layers, which explains their particular performance and stacking‑context behaviors. SVG filters extend this model by defining shader‑like filter graphs that operate on intermediate images, while CSS filters and blend modes represent a subset of these capabilities exposed through higher‑level syntax. [smashingmagazine](https://www.smashingmagazine.com/2016/05/web-image-effects-performance-showdown/)
32:Backdrop filtering conceptually inverts conventional filtering by sampling and filtering the pixels behind a given element, which allows frosted‑glass and depth effects that depend on the underlying content, rather than on a static texture. WebGL and WebGPU (although the latter lies mostly outside this survey’s evidence base) provide programmable shader pipelines capable of real‑time 2D or 3D scenes, which can be layered under or behind traditional DOM content through `<canvas>` or `<svg>` elements. Variable fonts, standardized in CSS Fonts modules, expose continuous axes (for example, weight, width, optical size) that can be animated or interpolated like other numeric CSS properties, integrating typography into the broader animation and interaction system. [web-platform-dx.github](https://web-platform-dx.github.io/web-features-explorer/features/backdrop-filter/)
48:| Shader‑driven canvases          | 4.7 WebGL / shader‑based backgrounds                  | GPU shader programs rendering into canvas layers        | Rich hero sections, particle fields |
55:**Theory & mechanism.** CSS blend modes generalize compositing by applying per‑pixel blending functions, such as multiply or screen, between overlapping layers, thereby modulating how colors from foreground and background combine. The `background-blend-mode` property blends multiple background layers and the element’s background color, whereas `mix-blend-mode` blends the element’s rendered content with whatever is behind it in the stacking context, which introduces stacking‑context and isolation subtleties. These mechanisms effectively bring traditional image‑editor blending workflows into the live rendering pipeline, enabling duotones, colorization, and contextual text treatments without pre‑baked assets. [developer.mozilla.org.cach3](https://developer.mozilla.org.cach3.com/tr/docs/Web/CSS/background-blend-mode)
57:**Literature evidence.** MDN documents `background-blend-mode` as part of the Compositing and Blending Level 1 specification, providing examples and early browser compatibility matrices. Can I Use tracks blend mode support across engines, indicating long‑standing implementation in Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge, with only legacy Internet Explorer lacking support, which positions blend modes as mature features by 2025. CSS‑Tricks’ reference on `mix-blend-mode` describes practical usage patterns, including text overlay blending and image compositing, reinforcing that these properties have entered mainstream front‑end practice. Smashing Magazine’s performance comparison of CSS filters, SVG filters, and CSS blend modes shows that blend modes render with similar timing to CSS filters in Chrome 47, suggesting modest performance overhead relative to baseline rendering. [caniuse](https://caniuse.com/?search=blend-mode)
59:**Implementations & benchmarks.** The Smashing Magazine study measured rendering and paint times for images processed with CSS filters, SVG filters, CSS blend modes, and canvas, finding that CSS blend modes incurred paint times around 4.45 ms compared with 4.28 ms for CSS filters and 5.80 ms for SVG filters under test conditions. This analysis suggests that blend modes sit in the middle of the filter performance spectrum, neither negligible nor prohibitive, especially when applied to a limited number of elements. Contemporary design systems, including many documented by MDN and CSS‑Tricks, use blend modes for duotone icons, background overlays, and hero imagery, with usage patterns stabilized around static or gently animated compositions. [css-tricks](https://css-tricks.com/almanac/properties/m/mix-blend-mode/)
69:**Implementations & benchmarks.** The Modern CSS Daily article illustrates `mask-image` applied to backgrounds combined with `mask-mode: alpha` and responsive sizing, emphasizing its ability to deliver intricate shapes with a single DOM element. TestMu’s dashboards, which collate engine support and weight it into a “browser compatibility score,” assign CSS masks a fully supported status for the majority of current browser versions, providing quantitative backing for classifying masking as production‑ready. Practical tutorials, including German‑language material, demonstrate that on unsupported browsers the mask has no effect while the underlying image or video remains visible, thereby documenting a de facto graceful degradation behavior aligned with the specification. [mediaevent](https://www.mediaevent.de/css/mask-image.html)
87:**Literature evidence.** TestMu AI’s 2026 browser compatibility dashboard assigns SVG filters a compatibility score of 97 out of 100, reporting full support in modern Chrome, Edge, Firefox, Safari, and Opera versions, with only very old Safari and Internet Explorer versions lacking support. Smashing Magazine’s “Web Image Effects Performance Showdown” compares CSS filters, SVG filters, CSS blend modes, and canvas operations, concluding that CSS filters, SVG filters, and CSS blend modes show similar load and render times, with SVG filters slightly slower but still within acceptable ranges for many use cases. A 2025 Frontend Masters blog on grainy gradients describes two implementations of grain: an SVG filter applied to a gradient and a CSS `filter: url(#grain)` applied to a container, showcasing that procedural noise can be integrated with both CSS gradients and SVG masking pipelines. An optimization guide for SVG animations emphasizes that filters, particularly blurs and shadows, are expensive to render and should be used sparingly, especially in animated contexts and on mobile devices. [smashingmagazine](https://www.smashingmagazine.com/2016/05/web-image-effects-performance-showdown/)
111:**Strengths & limitations.** Backdrop filtering shows strong evidence of cross‑browser maturity among evergreen browsers, enabling dynamic, content‑aware depth and glass effects without resorting to duplicated background layers or canvas rendering. At the same time, its implementation can introduce performance overhead due to repeated sampling and blurring of large background regions, and browser‑specific quirks (for example, overflow interactions in Chrome) require careful design to avoid visual glitches. Authors therefore pair backdrop filters with progressive enhancement, feature queries, and mask‑based optimizations (for example, limiting the filtered area via `mask-image`) to confine cost to small, visually important regions, while users on unsupported browsers see conventional opaque or semi‑transparent panels. [web-platform-dx.github](https://web-platform-dx.github.io/web-features-explorer/features/backdrop-filter/)
113:### 4.7 WebGL / Shader‑Based Backgrounds
115:**Theory & mechanism.** WebGL exposes a programmable graphics pipeline based on OpenGL ES, enabling vertex and fragment shaders to render arbitrary 2D or 3D scenes into `<canvas>` elements, which can then be composited with DOM content using standard layering and positioning mechanisms. In visual website design, this capability underpins particle systems, fluid simulations, procedural landscapes, and interactive 3D objects that serve as dynamic backgrounds or hero‑section canvases. Shaders operate on a per‑vertex or per‑pixel basis, allowing tight control over lighting, noise, and material appearance that exceeds what CSS filters and gradients currently express.
117:**Literature evidence.** While the present tool‑gathered corpus focuses more on CSS and SVG, broader web standards documentation and long‑standing industry practice identify WebGL as a mature, widely implemented standard across major desktop and mobile browsers, with deprecation largely confined to extremely old engines. Three.js, Babylon.js, and similar libraries have for over a decade demonstrated stable abstractions over WebGL for scene management, easing adoption for visual experiences that need real‑time 3D or sophisticated procedural backgrounds. These practices are consistent with more recent performance research, which emphasizes the importance of GPU‑accelerated rendering and careful management of draw calls and buffer updates for responsive experiences.
119:**Implementations & benchmarks.** In contemporary front‑end ecosystems, shader‑based backgrounds typically appear as Three.js scenes rendered into full‑window `<canvas>` elements, with DOM content layered above using `position: fixed` or `position: relative` stacking contexts. Many production sites constrain WebGL use to a single scene or hero section, limiting GPU and battery load while still leveraging advanced lighting or particle animations for visual differentiation. Performance tends to depend more on shader complexity, object counts, and fill rate than on the mere use of WebGL itself, leading to patterns that prioritize simple geometry, batched draw calls, and resolution scaling on low‑power devices.
121:**Strengths & limitations.** WebGL and shader‑based approaches offer unmatched expressiveness for complex backgrounds and interactions, including volumetric lighting, real‑time reflections, or physically inspired particles, which CSS and SVG cannot currently match. However, they introduce an entirely separate rendering stack, higher implementation complexity, and potentially significant GPU and power usage, particularly on mobile hardware. Accessibility and robustness considerations also differ, since canvas content is not inherently semantic, which requires careful overlay of HTML for readable text and controls, plus integration with reduced‑motion and reduced‑data preferences where intense animation would otherwise dominate the experience.
131:**Strengths & limitations.** Variable font animation integrates typography into the broader CSS animation system without introducing new rendering subsystems, which simplifies composition with layout, color, and filter effects. However, legibility and accessibility impose stricter constraints on the extent and speed of typographic motion than on purely decorative backgrounds, especially for body text or long passages. Support for variable fonts is strong in modern browsers but still depends on authoring fonts with appropriate axes; older fonts or limited axis designs may constrain the achievable range of animated expression.
145:| 4.7 WebGL / shader backgrounds         | High for WebGL; widely implemented in major browsers.        | Potentially high GPU and power usage; depends on scene complexity. | High; requires shader or library expertise. | Requires overlayed semantic content; strong considerations for motion sensitivity and device capability. | High; mature libraries like Three.js and Babylon.js. |
156:- **Filter and backdrop performance characterization on mobile.** Existing performance studies comparing CSS filters, SVG filters, blend modes, and canvas operate on desktop‑class hardware and limited scenarios, leaving uncertainty about filter and backdrop‑filter behavior under mobile GPU constraints and complex layouts. Systematic benchmarks across devices, combined with guidance in platform documentation, would clarify safe operational envelopes for these effects. [smashingmagazine](https://www.smashingmagazine.com/2016/05/web-image-effects-performance-showdown/)
166:This survey documents a layered ecosystem of browser‑native technologies that collectively enable sophisticated, visually distinctive websites using combinations of CSS compositing, masking, gradients, SVG filter pipelines, scroll‑linked animations, backdrop compositions, shader‑driven canvases, and variable font animations. General gradients, blend modes, CSS masks, SVG filters, backdrop filters, WebGL backgrounds, and variable fonts exhibit strong evidence of cross‑browser maturity, backed by MDN reference data, compatibility dashboards, and long‑running production use, whereas conic gradients and scroll‑timeline‑based animations appear in more transitional stages with uneven adoption and evolving specifications. [developer.mozilla.org.cach3](https://developer.mozilla.org.cach3.com/tr/docs/Web/CSS/background-blend-mode)

12:Digital Audio Workstations (DAWs) represent one of the most architecturally complex categories of professional software, requiring sub-millisecond timing precision, deterministic undo/redo across heterogeneous state, multi-threaded real-time audio processing, and scalable project management for sessions containing thousands of audio events. This survey examines the canonical design patterns employed in professional DAWs — specifically Ableton Live, Logic Pro X, and Reaper — across seven architectural domains: transport system design, non-destructive editing models, signal routing topology, mixer architecture, session/project serialization, arrangement view rendering, and MIDI subsystems. Each domain is analyzed from both theoretical and implementation perspectives, with concrete data structures and algorithms documented in TypeScript/pseudocode. This document serves as primary reference material for AI agents building a browser-based DAW with React and the Web Audio API.

316:**SMPTE/MTC** (MIDI Time Code) is used for synchronization with video and film hardware, encoding hours/minutes/seconds/frames as quarter-frame MIDI messages.

638:**Ableton Live** terminology: "Audio Track", "MIDI Track", "Return Track", "Master Track". Group tracks are created by nesting.
640:**Logic Pro**: "Audio", "Software Instrument", "MIDI External", "Aux", "Master", "Summing Stack Sub". Summing stacks are Logic's unique model where grouped tracks feed a hidden summing bus automatically.
642:**Reaper**: No fixed track types. Every track can receive any combination of audio/MIDI and route to any other track. Track type is determined by what is inserted on it (instruments convert MIDI to audio).

1345	## 9. MIDI Subsystem
1346	
1347	### 9.1 MIDI Event Model
1348	
1349	```typescript
1350	type MidiEventType =
1351	  | 'note-on'
1352	  | 'note-off'
1353	  | 'control-change'
1354	  | 'program-change'
1355	  | 'pitch-bend'
1356	  | 'aftertouch'
1357	  | 'poly-aftertouch'
1358	  | 'sysex';
1359	
1360	interface MidiEvent {
1361	  type: MidiEventType;
1362	  channel: number;      // 1-16
1363	  timestamp: number;    // beats from session start (for stored events)
1364	                        // or AudioContext.currentTime (for live events)
1365	}
1366	
1367	interface NoteOnEvent extends MidiEvent {
1368	  type: 'note-on';
1369	  note: number;         // 0-127 (60 = middle C)
1370	  velocity: number;     // 0-127
1371	}
1372	
1373	interface NoteOffEvent extends MidiEvent {
1374	  type: 'note-off';
1375	  note: number;
1376	  velocity: number;     // often ignored, sometimes used for release time
1377	}
1378	
1379	interface ControlChangeEvent extends MidiEvent {
1380	  type: 'control-change';
1381	  controller: number;   // 0-127 (e.g., 7=volume, 10=pan, 64=sustain)
1382	  value: number;        // 0-127
1383	}
1384	
1385	interface PitchBendEvent extends MidiEvent {
1386	  type: 'pitch-bend';
1387	  value: number;        // -8192 to +8191 (0 = center)
1388	}
1389	```
1390	
1391	MIDI notes are commonly stored as start/end pairs rather than note-on/note-off pairs, since the duration is the musically relevant attribute:
1392	
1393	```typescript
1394	interface MidiNote {
1395	  id: string;
1396	  note: number;
1397	  velocity: number;
1398	  startBeats: number;
1399	  durationBeats: number;
1400	  channel: number;
1401	}
1402	```
1403	
1404	### 9.2 Piano Roll Data Model
1405	
1406	The piano roll is the primary editor for MIDI clips. Its data model is a set of `MidiNote` objects within a clip's beat range.
1407	
1408	```typescript
1409	interface MidiClip extends BaseClip {
1410	  notes: MidiNote[];
1411	  controllerData: ControllerLane[];   // per-controller automation
1412	  pitchBendData: AutomationPoint[];
1413	  tempoOverride?: number;             // clip-specific tempo (Ableton)
1414	  loopEnabled: boolean;
1415	  loopStartBeats: number;
1416	  loopEndBeats: number;
1417	}
1418	
1419	interface ControllerLane {
1420	  controller: number;
1421	  points: AutomationPoint[];
1422	}
1423	```
1424	
1425	Piano roll viewport state mirrors the arrangement view but uses note pitch (MIDI note number) on the Y axis:
1426	
1427	```typescript
1428	interface PianoRollViewport {
1429	  scrollBeats: number;
1430	  pixelsPerBeat: number;
1431	  scrollNote: number;     // MIDI note at top of viewport
1432	  pixelsPerNote: number;  // typically 12-20 px per semitone
1433	  canvasWidth: number;
1434	  canvasHeight: number;
1435	}
1436	```
1437	
1438	### 9.3 Quantization Algorithms
1439	
1440	Quantization snaps note start times (and optionally durations) to a grid.
1441	
1442	#### 9.3.1 Hard Quantize
1443	
1444	```typescript
1445	function quantizeNote(note: MidiNote, gridBeats: number, strength: number = 1.0): MidiNote {
1446	  const snapped = Math.round(note.startBeats / gridBeats) * gridBeats;
1447	  const newStart = note.startBeats + (snapped - note.startBeats) * strength;
1448	  return { ...note, startBeats: newStart };
1449	}
1450	```
1451	
1452	#### 9.3.2 Groove Quantize
1453	
1454	Groove quantize uses a template (a "groove" extracted from a drum loop or predefined grid) to impose a rhythmic feel. Each note is snapped to the nearest groove grid point:
1455	
1456	```typescript
1457	interface GrooveTemplate {
1458	  gridBeats: number;           // base grid size
1459	  offsets: Float32Array;       // per-subdivision timing offsets in beats
1460	  velocities: Float32Array;    // per-subdivision velocity scaling
1461	}
1462	
1463	function grooveQuantize(note: MidiNote, groove: GrooveTemplate, strength: number): MidiNote {
1464	  const subdivIndex = Math.round(note.startBeats / groove.gridBeats) % groove.offsets.length;
1465	  const targetBeat = Math.round(note.startBeats / groove.gridBeats) * groove.gridBeats
1466	    + groove.offsets[subdivIndex];
1467	  const newStart = note.startBeats + (targetBeat - note.startBeats) * strength;
1468	  const newVelocity = note.velocity * (1 + (groove.velocities[subdivIndex] - 1) * strength);
1469	  return { ...note, startBeats: newStart, velocity: Math.round(newVelocity) };
1470	}
1471	```
1472	
1473	**Ableton Live** supports extracting groove from any audio clip (extracting its transient timing). **Logic Pro** has built-in groove templates ("Swing", "Shuffle") and supports importing MIDI as a groove. **Reaper** has quantize-to-groove via scripts.
1474	
1475	### 9.4 MIDI Recording with Latency Compensation
1476	
1477	MIDI input events arrive from the system MIDI stack with a timestamp. This timestamp must be corrected for:
1478	
1479	1. **Input latency**: time between key press and the event reaching the DAW
1480	2. **Output latency**: time between audio engine processing and reaching speakers
1481	
1482	The total latency is `inputLatency + outputLatency`. When recording, subtract this from the event timestamp to get the corrected musical position:
1483	
1484	```typescript
1485	function correctMidiEventLatency(
1486	  event: MidiEvent,
1487	  audioContext: AudioContext,
1488	  inputLatencySeconds: number
1489	): number {
1490	  // event.timestamp is DOMHighResTimeStamp from Web MIDI API
1491	  const eventAudioTime = event.timestamp / 1000 - audioContext.baseLatency - inputLatencySeconds;
1492	  const outputLatency = audioContext.outputLatency;
1493	  const correctedAudioTime = eventAudioTime - outputLatency;
1494	  return audioContextTimeToBeats(correctedAudioTime, tempoMap);
1495	}
1496	```
1497	
1498	**Ableton Live** pioneered "Low Latency Monitoring" which uses the ASIO driver's reported latency for precise compensation. **Logic Pro** uses Core Audio's reported latency. Browser DAWs use `AudioContext.baseLatency` and `AudioContext.outputLatency` (both available in modern browsers).
1499	
1500	### 9.5 MIDI Clip Recording Pipeline
1501	
1502	```
1503	Web MIDI API input event
1504	  → timestamp correction (latency compensation)
1505	  → beat position calculation
1506	  → note-on: create NoteOn record with openNoteMap[note] = { id, startBeats, velocity }
1507	  → note-off: close note from openNoteMap, compute durationBeats, add MidiNote to clip
1508	  → real-time UI update (show notes appearing in piano roll)
1509	```
1510	
1511	The `openNoteMap` handles the case where note-on and note-off are processed in separate audio callbacks.
1512
