1514	
1515	## 10. Comparative Synthesis
1516	
1517	### 10.1 Architectural Comparison Table
1518	
1519	| Dimension | Ableton Live 12 | Logic Pro 11 | Reaper 7 | Browser DAW (Web) |
1520	|---|---|---|---|---|
1521	| **Primary time unit** | Beats (float64) | Ticks (480 PPQN) | Samples + beats | Beats (float64) |
1522	| **Clip model** | Clip with no takes | Region (take stacks) | Item + N takes | AudioClip (see §4.1) |
1523	| **Undo model** | State snapshot delta | Command (forward/inverse) | Command (forward/inverse) | Immer snapshots |
1524	| **Routing model** | Fixed track types | Flexible aux routing | Any-to-any matrix | Graph with typed edges |
1525	| **Solo model** | SIP | SIP + Implicit | SIP + AFL/PFL | SIP (configurable) |
1526	| **Automation** | Envelope segments | Hyper Draw regions | Per-point per-segment | Breakpoint lanes |
1527	| **MIDI quantize** | Groove extraction | Logic groove templates | Script-based | Hard + groove |
1528	| **Session format** | Binary (.als = ZIP + XML) | Package directory (.logicx) | RPP (text, key-value) | JSON |
1529	| **Auto-save** | Yes (30s) | Yes (30s crash recovery) | Undo history backup | IndexedDB |
1530	| **VCA groups** | No | Yes | Yes | Implementation choice |
1531	| **Sidechain** | Post-fader only | Pre/post selectable | Any tap point | AudioWorklet custom |
1532	
1533	### 10.2 Transport System Trade-offs
1534	
1535	| Approach | Accuracy | Complexity | Flexibility |
1536	|---|---|---|---|
1537	| Fixed BPM only | Sample-exact | Low | Low (no tempo changes) |
1538	| Tempo map (constant segments) | Sample-exact | Medium | High |
1539	| Linear tempo ramps | Sub-sample error (float) | Medium-high | Very high |
1540	| Beat-clock (no absolute sample) | Beat-accurate | Low | Medium |
1541	
1542	For a browser DAW, a tempo map with constant-BPM segments is the correct starting point. Linear ramps add significant complexity and are needed only for expressive tempo automation.
1543	
1544	### 10.3 Undo/Redo Strategy Trade-offs
1545	
1546	| Strategy | Memory | Implementation complexity | Correctness risk |
1547	|---|---|---|---|
1548	| Full state snapshots | O(N * history_depth) | Low | Low |
1549	| Structural sharing (Immer) | O(delta * history_depth) | Low-medium | Low |
1550	| Forward/inverse commands | O(delta * history_depth) | High | Medium (inverse bugs) |
1551	| CRDT-based | O(operations) | Very high | Low |
1552	
1553	For a browser DAW, Immer with structural sharing is the pragmatic choice: React and TypeScript make the immutable state model natural, the overhead is acceptable for sessions up to ~10,000 clips, and the implementation is far simpler than maintaining correct inverse operations for every edit type.
1554	
1555	### 10.4 Signal Routing Graph Trade-offs
1556	
1557	| Model | Flexibility | Complexity | Cycle detection |
1558	|---|---|---|---|
1559	| Fixed track type hierarchy | Low | Low | Impossible by design |
1560	| Soft-typed routing (Reaper) | Maximum | High | Required at every connection |
1561	| Summing stacks (Logic) | Medium | Medium | Required |
1562	| Any-to-any with type checks | High | Medium-high | Required |
1563	
1564	---
1565	
1566	## 11. Open Problems and Unsolved Challenges
1567	
1568	### 11.1 Web Audio Timing Precision
1569	
1570	The Web Audio API's `AudioContext.currentTime` has been documented to have jitter on certain platforms (Android Chrome, Safari on lower-powered devices). For a production browser DAW, this requires:
1571	
1572	- Testing on all target platforms
1573	- Fallback to `performance.now()` for scheduling with appropriate fudge factor
1574	- Using `AudioWorkletProcessor` for truly sample-accurate scheduling rather than relying on `currentTime`
1575	
1576	### 11.2 Multi-Track Recording Synchronization
1577	
1578	Recording multiple tracks simultaneously requires that all input streams (from the same `MediaStream` source) are sample-aligned. The Web Audio API provides no guarantee of alignment between separate `MediaStreamAudioSourceNode` instances connected to the same `AudioContext`. This is a known limitation for browser-based multi-track recording.
1579	
1580	### 11.3 Plugin (Audio Worklet) Latency Compensation
1581	
1582	When a plugin (AudioWorkletNode) introduces lookahead processing (e.g., a look-ahead compressor), the DAW must delay all other tracks by the same amount to maintain alignment. This **plugin latency compensation (PDC)** is a complex graph-level problem:
1583	
1584	- Reaper and Logic Pro implement automatic PDC
1585	- Ableton Live has manual PDC per plugin
1586	- Web Audio has no native PDC mechanism; it must be implemented in the routing layer by inserting `DelayNode` instances with the correct delay value
1587	
1588	### 11.4 Large Session Performance
1589	
1590	At 200+ tracks with complex routing, the JavaScript audio graph management overhead can become a bottleneck. Options include:
1591	
1592	- Moving graph traversal to an `AudioWorkletProcessor` (runs off main thread)
1593	- Using SharedArrayBuffer to share state between main thread and audio worklet
1594	- Compiling the graph to a flat series of operations (like LLVM IR) for the worklet
1595	
1596	### 11.5 Offline Rendering (Bounce/Export)
1597	
1598	Web Audio's `OfflineAudioContext` enables offline rendering at faster-than-real-time. However, `AudioWorklet` processors do not run in `OfflineAudioContext` in all browsers (historically a WebKit limitation). This means custom processing that relies on AudioWorklet may not be usable for offline bounce.
1599	
1600	### 11.6 MIDI 2.0
1601	
1602	The MIDI 2.0 standard (ratified 2020) introduces:
1603	
1604	- 32-bit resolution for controllers (vs 7-bit in MIDI 1.0)
1605	- Per-note controllers
1606	- Bidirectional property exchange (MIDI-CI)
1607	
1608	Web MIDI API currently exposes MIDI 1.0 only. MIDI 2.0 browser support is not yet standardized. A DAW designed today should use 32-bit internal precision for controller data and render down to MIDI 1.0 for output.
1609	
1610	---
1611	
1612	## 12. Implementation Recommendations for Browser DAW
1613	
1614	This section distills the survey into concrete architectural recommendations for a React + TypeScript + Web Audio API DAW.
1615	
1616	### 12.1 State Architecture
1617	
1618	Use a single immutable session state object with Immer:
1619	
1620	```typescript
1621	// store.ts
1622	import { create } from 'zustand';
1623	import { immer } from 'zustand/middleware/immer';
1624	
1625	type SessionStore = {
1626	  session: Session;
1627	  past: Session[];
1628	  future: Session[];
1629	  dispatch: (command: Command<Session>) => void;
1630	  undo: () => void;
1631	  redo: () => void;
1632	};
1633	```
1634	
1635	### 12.2 Audio Engine Separation
1636	
1637	Keep the audio engine (`AudioContext`, `AudioWorklet`, all `AudioNode` instances) completely separate from React state. Use a singleton `AudioEngine` class that:
1638	
1639	- Subscribes to session state changes via a diffing subscription
1640	- Updates the audio graph in response to structural changes (track add/remove, routing changes)
1641	- Exposes a `getPlayheadBeats(audioCtx: AudioContext): number` method for cursor rendering
1642	- Communicates recording events to the React layer via a message queue, not direct state mutation
1643	
1644	### 12.3 Transport Hook
1645	
1646	```typescript
1647	// useTransport.ts
1648	function useTransport(): TransportControls {
1649	  const audioEngine = useAudioEngine();
1650	  const { session, dispatch } = useSessionStore();
1651	
1652	  return {
1653	    play: () => audioEngine.play(),
1654	    stop: () => audioEngine.stop(),
1655	    record: () => audioEngine.record(),
1656	    seek: (beats: number) => audioEngine.seek(beats),
1657	    setTempo: (bpm: number) => dispatch(new SetTempoCommand(bpm)),
1658	    playheadBeats: usePlayheadBeats(audioEngine),  // animated float64
1659	  };
1660	}
1661	```
1662	
1663	### 12.4 Clip Rendering
1664	
1665	Use `<canvas>` for the arrangement view rather than DOM elements for clips. At 1000 clips, DOM-based rendering produces layout thrashing and paint overhead that the canvas approach avoids entirely.
1666	
1667	For waveform rendering, pre-compute peak/RMS data at multiple resolution levels (peak pyramids) during audio file import:
1668	
1669	```typescript
1670	interface WaveformData {
1671	  sampleRate: number;
1672	  levels: WaveformLevel[];  // level[0] = 1 sample/pixel at 1:1 zoom, level[n] = 2^n samples/pixel
1673	}
1674	
1675	interface WaveformLevel {
1676	  peaks: Float32Array;   // max abs sample per pixel-bucket
1677	  rms: Float32Array;     // RMS per pixel-bucket
1678	}
1679	```
1680	
1681	This approach (used by all three DAWs) enables O(1) waveform rendering at any zoom level.
1682	
1683	---
1684	
1685	## 13. Conclusion
1686	
1687	DAW architecture represents a mature set of engineering patterns accumulated over four decades of commercial development. The core patterns — tempo maps for beat/sample conversion, the command pattern for invertible edits, the audio graph for signal routing, and immutable session data for serialization — are well-understood and have clear implementations in TypeScript and the Web Audio API.
1688	
1689	The primary challenges for browser-based DAWs are not algorithmic but environmental: Web Audio timing jitter, multi-track recording synchronization, plugin delay compensation, and offline rendering coverage. Each has known workarounds (AudioWorkletProcessor scheduling, OfflineAudioContext with careful plugin selection, manual PDC configuration) that are acceptable for an initial implementation.
1690	
1691	The reference architecture synthesized in this survey — Immer-based immutable session state, command pattern undo/redo, AudioWorklet-based render engine, canvas arrangement view with peak pyramids, and JSON session format with relative media pool paths — is the pragmatic path to a production-quality browser DAW.
1692

276:| NFR-04 | Accessibility | All interactive controls shall be keyboard-navigable (WCAG 2.2 AA) |
282:| NFR-10 | Testability | All DSP algorithms shall be testable via OfflineAudioContext (deterministic rendering) |
285:| NFR-13 | Rendering | High-frequency UI updates (metering, waveforms, playback cursor, piano roll) shall bypass React state and render directly to HTML5 Canvas or mutate DOM via useRef, reading from SharedArrayBuffer via requestAnimationFrame. |
288:| NFR-16 | Input Validation | All MIDI input values shall be clamped to valid ranges (note/CC: 0-127, pitch bend: 0-16383). SysEx messages capped at 1KB. All external input treated as untrusted. |
289:| NFR-17 | Export | Bounce/export shall use chunked OfflineAudioContext rendering (30-second blocks) with incremental WAV encoding to avoid single large buffer allocation. |

Now let me compile a comprehensive summary of all the relevant findings:

## Summary of Relevant Documentation

I've completed a thorough search of the `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/` directory. Here are all the relevant files found:

### ADRs (Architecture Decision Records)

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/adr/0001-initial-tooling-choices.md`

Full content provided above. This ADR documents the decision to use `beads` for issue tracking and `compound-agent` for session memory.

---

### EARS Requirements and Specifications

**Main Specification File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/specs/brutalwav-daw.md`

This is the comprehensive BRUTALWAV Digital Audio Workstation specification containing:

#### Relevant EARS Requirements Found:

**R-EVT-04** (Event-Driven): "When the user triggers a MIDI note (keyboard/mouse on piano roll or virtual keyboard) | The system shall allocate a synth voice, apply the current patch parameters (oscillators, filter, envelopes, LFOs), and route through the instrument track's signal chain within 10ms"

**R-EVT-17** (Event-Driven): "When the user presses a keyboard shortcut | The system shall execute the mapped command without requiring mouse interaction"

**R-OPT-01** (Optional/Feature): "Where MIDI input device is connected | The system shall accept MIDI note and CC messages, routing them to the selected instrument track"

**NFR-04** (Non-Functional): "Accessibility | All interactive controls shall be keyboard-navigable (WCAG 2.2 AA)"

**NFR-13** (Non-Functional - CRITICAL for Canvas Rendering): "Rendering | High-frequency UI updates (metering, waveforms, playback cursor, piano roll) shall bypass React state and render directly to HTML5 Canvas or mutate DOM via useRef, reading from SharedArrayBuffer via requestAnimationFrame."

**NFR-16** (Non-Functional): "Input Validation | All MIDI input values shall be clamped to valid ranges (note/CC: 0-127, pitch bend: 0-16383). SysEx messages capped at 1KB. All external input treated as untrusted."

---

### Advisory Brief

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/specs/brutalwav-daw-advisory-brief.md`

Contains critical advisory concerns from three expert reviewers:

- **P2 Concern #17** (Scalability): "DOM overload in piano roll and arrangement | Thousands of MIDI notes and waveform SVGs will overwhelm the layout engine | Action: Use HTML5 Canvas or WebGL for timeline, waveforms, and piano roll."

This directly supports NFR-13's canvas rendering requirement.

---

### DAW Design Patterns Research

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/research/development/web-audio/daw-design-patterns.md`

Contains detailed specifications for:

- **Piano Roll Data Model** (Section 9.2): 
  - MidiClip interface with notes array
  - PianoRollViewport state with scrollBeats, pixelsPerBeat, scrollNote, pixelsPerNote
  - Supports 12-20 px per semitone rendering

- **Arrangement View Rendering** (Section 8.1-8.3):
  - 2D canvas with X axis = time, Y axis = tracks
  - ViewportState: scrollBeats, pixelsPerBeat, trackHeightPx
  - Spatial indexing (interval tree) for viewport culling at 10,000+ clips
  - Zoom-to-point functionality
  - Snap-to-grid and magnetic snap algorithms

- **MIDI Note Model**:
  - Stored as start/end pairs rather than note-on/note-off
  - Includes ID, note number (0-127), velocity, startBeats, durationBeats, channel

- **Clip Recording Pipeline**: Web MIDI API → latency compensation → beat position → openNoteMap handling

---

### Canvas & Frontend Design Research

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/research/design/frontend-design/advanced-css-and-wbgl-visual-craft.md`

Contains extensive information on:
- WebGL/Canvas rendering approaches
- Performance implications of DOM vs Canvas
- Blend modes, masking, SVG filters
- Scroll-linked animations
- Backdrop filtering

---

### Web Audio API Architecture Research

**File**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/research/development/web-audio/web-audio-api-architecture.md`

(File too large to fully read, but contains foundational architecture patterns)

---

## Key Findings Summary

### Piano Roll & MIDI-Related Requirements:
- R-EVT-04: 10ms latency requirement for MIDI note triggering
- R-EVT-17: Keyboard shortcut execution
- R-OPT-01: MIDI device input handling
- NFR-16: Input validation for MIDI values (0-127 for notes/CC, 0-16383 for pitch bend)

### Canvas Rendering (Critical):
- **NFR-13** explicitly mandates Canvas/direct DOM for high-frequency updates
- Specifies requestAnimationFrame + SharedArrayBuffer reading pattern
- Applies to: metering, waveforms, playback cursor, **piano roll**
- Advisory brief confirms DOM bottleneck at 1000+ clips
- DAW patterns document recommends spatial indexing for viewport culling

### Editor Design:
- Piano roll uses note pitch (MIDI 0-127) on Y axis
- Viewport state tracking (scroll position, pixels per unit, canvas dimensions)
- Supports multiple zoom levels with peak pyramid waveform data
- Snap-to-grid with magnetic snap (8px threshold default)

### Performance Requirements:
- 16 simultaneous tracks with effects at 60fps (NFR-02)
- 16-voice polyphony without glitches (NFR-03)
- Sessions with 50 tracks save/load within 2 seconds (NFR-08)
- < 20ms audio output latency (NFR-01)

All relevant files have been identified and their full content provided above.

Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me create a detailed analysis document:

## Comprehensive DAW Data Model Analysis for MIDI/Piano Roll Extension

### 1. CURRENT DATA MODEL STRUCTURE

#### **ClipModel (src/state/track/types.ts)**
```typescript
type ClipModel = {
  readonly id: string;              // Unique clip identifier
  readonly trackId: string;          // Parent track reference
  readonly sourceId: string;         // Reference to audio source in media pool
  readonly startTime: number;        // Timeline position (seconds)
  readonly sourceOffset: number;     // Offset into source (seconds)
  readonly duration: number;         // Clip duration (seconds)
  readonly gain: number;             // Clip volume (0..2)
  readonly fadeIn: number;           // Fade in duration (seconds)
  readonly fadeOut: number;          // Fade out duration (seconds)
  readonly name: string;             // Display name
};
```

**Key observations:**
- Currently only supports **audio clips** with media pool sources
- `sourceId` points to audio files, not applicable to MIDI
- `sourceOffset` is for seeking within audio sources
- No note/event data structure exists
- Linear/flat properties; immutable throughout

#### **TrackModel (src/state/track/types.ts)**
```typescript
type TrackModel = {
  readonly id: string;
  readonly name: string;
  readonly type: TrackType;          // "audio" | "instrument"
  readonly color: string;            // #RRGGBB format
  readonly muted: boolean;
  readonly solo: boolean;
  readonly armed: boolean;
  readonly soloIsolate: boolean;
  readonly volume: number;           // 0..2
  readonly pan: number;              // -1..1
  readonly clipIds: readonly string[];  // Child clip references
};
```

**Key observations:**
- Already has `type: "instrument"` support but not utilized for MIDI
- `clipIds` array maintains order and composition of clips
- No per-track MIDI routing or instrument configuration
- Tracks are container-agnostic (could hold audio or MIDI clips)

---

### 2. STORE STRUCTURE (src/state/store.ts - useDawStore)

**Architecture Pattern:** Zustand store with immutable state updates

#### **Clip Management Methods:**
```
addClip(clip)              → Adds clip to store + updates track.clipIds
removeClip(id)             → Removes clip + updates track.clipIds
moveClip(id, time, track?) → Updates startTime and/or trackId
splitClip(id, atTime)      → Creates two clips, returns right clip ID
trimClip(id, start, end)   → Adjusts startTime/duration/sourceOffset
duplicateClip(id)          → Creates copy at startTime + duration
```

**Critical detail:** When clips are moved/added, the `clipIds` array on Track is automatically synchronized. This is a **referential integrity pattern** that must be maintained for MIDI clips too.

#### **State Shape:**
```typescript
{
  tracks: TrackModel[];
  clips: Record<string, ClipModel>;     // Map for O(1) lookup
  selectedClipIds: string[];
  selectedTrackIds: string[];
  // ... transport, mixer state
}
```

---

### 3. UNDO SYSTEM (src/state/undo/)

#### **UndoCommand Interface (undo-command.ts)**
```typescript
type UndoCommand = {
  readonly type: string;
  execute(): void;
  undo(): void;
  serialize(): Record<string, unknown>;  // For persistence
};
```

#### **Implementation Pattern (track-commands.ts):**
Each command **captures state before execution** and can deterministically undo:

**Example: MoveClipCommand**
1. **execute()** → Captures old clip state, calls `store.moveClip()`
2. **undo()** → Calls `store.moveClip()` with saved oldStartTime/oldTrackId
3. **serialize()** → Returns record of command parameters

**Key insight:** Commands don't store full before/after snapshots for efficiency—they store only what's needed to reverse the operation. For destructive operations (split, trim), they capture the full original clip.

#### **UndoManager (undo-manager.ts)**
- Stack-based undo/redo with max size 200
- `push()` clears redo stack (standard behavior)
- Accessed via `sharedUndoManager` singleton

---

### 4. CANVAS RENDERING & INTERACTION PATTERN

#### **Rendering Pipeline (arrangement-renderer.ts)**

Pure canvas renderer with **6 distinct stages:**
1. `drawBackground()` – Gray background
2. `drawTimeRuler()` – Bar numbers based on BPM
3. `drawGrid()` – Time grid (beat lines) with smart step calculation
4. `drawTrackLanes()` – Divider lines between tracks
5. `drawTrackHeaders()` – Track names, color strip, M/S indicators
6. `drawClips()` – Clip rectangles with names
7. `drawPlayhead()` – Red cursor line

**RenderContext (passed to renderer):**
```typescript
{
  ctx: CanvasRenderingContext2D;
  width, height: number;
  view: ArrangementViewState;     // Scroll, zoom, layout
  tracks: TrackModel[];
  clips: Record<string, ClipModel>;
  selectedClipIds: string[];
  cursorSeconds: number;
  bpm: number;
}
```

**Key coordinate transformations:**
```
secondsToX(seconds) = headerWidth + (seconds - scrollX) * pixelsPerSecond
trackY(index) = RULER_HEIGHT + index * trackHeight - scrollY
```

#### **Hit Testing (hit-test.ts)**

Converts pixel coordinates to semantic targets:
```typescript
type HitResult =
  | { kind: "none" }
  | { kind: "track-header"; trackIndex; trackId }
  | { kind: "clip-body"; clipId; trackId }
  | { kind: "clip-left-edge"; clipId; trackId }    // Trim handles
  | { kind: "clip-right-edge"; clipId; trackId }
  | { kind: "ruler"; timeSeconds }
  | { kind: "empty-lane"; trackIndex; trackId }
```

**Grid snapping:**
```typescript
snapToGrid(seconds, bpm, snap: "1-bar" | "1/4" | "1/8" | "1/16")
```
Converts seconds to nearest grid line.

#### **Interaction Handler (use-arrangement-interactions.ts)**

**Drag state machine:**
```
idle → pointerDown (hit-test) → move-clip | trim-left | trim-right | rubber-band
       → pointerMove (continuous update to store)
       → pointerUp (create undo command)
```

**Critical pattern:** 
- Captures `beforeClip` on drag start
- Updates store **during** drag (live feedback)
- On drag end, creates undo command comparing before/after
- Uses `setPointerCapture()` for reliable drag tracking

**Double-click handling:**
- On clip-body: Creates `SplitClipCommand` at cursor time

---

### 5. SYNTH INTEGRATION (For Reference)

The codebase already has note-based synthesis:

#### **SynthVoiceCommand (synth-types.ts)**
```typescript
type SynthVoiceCommand =
  | { type: "noteOn"; note: number; velocity: number; legato: boolean }
  | { type: "noteOff"; note: number }
  | { type: "allNotesOff" }
  | { type: "setParam"; key: string; value: number }
```

**MIDI note → frequency conversion already exists:**
```typescript
function midiToFreq(note: number): number {
  return 440 * Math.pow(2, (note - 69) / 12);
}
```

This shows the expected MIDI range is 0-127 (standard MIDI).

---

### 6. SESSION PERSISTENCE (session-schema.ts)

The session schema currently serializes:
```
tracks: TrackSchema[]
clips: ClipSchema[]  // Flat array, not nested
```

**Schema is versioned** (SESSION_VERSION = 1), allowing future migrations.

---

## WHAT NEEDS TO CHANGE FOR MIDI/PIANO ROLL

### **Option A: Extend ClipModel (Recommended)**

Create a **union type** that allows clips to contain different content:

```typescript
type ClipModel = AudioClipModel | MidiClipModel;

type AudioClipModel = {
  readonly type: "audio";
  readonly id: string;
  readonly trackId: string;
  readonly sourceId: string;        // Media pool reference
  readonly startTime: number;
  readonly sourceOffset: number;
  readonly duration: number;
  // ... gain, fade, name
};

type MidiClipModel = {
  readonly type: "midi";
  readonly id: string;
  readonly trackId: string;
  readonly startTime: number;
  readonly duration: number;
  readonly noteEvents: readonly NoteEvent[];  // NEW
  readonly name: string;
  // No sourceId, sourceOffset, gain, fade (different semantics)
};

type NoteEvent = {
  readonly id: string;              // For individual note editing
  readonly startTime: number;       // Seconds (relative to clip.startTime)
  readonly duration: number;        // Seconds
  readonly pitch: number;           // MIDI note 0-127
  readonly velocity: number;        // 0-127
};
```

**Advantages:**
- Type-safe discrimination (clip.type determines which fields are valid)
- Reuses existing clip infrastructure (move, trim, split, duplicate)
- Single clips array works for both

**Disadvantages:**
- trim/split logic must be aware of clip type
- Store methods need to handle both cases

### **Option B: Separate MIDI Track Extension**

Add parallel MIDI data structure to TrackModel:
```typescript
type TrackModel = {
  // ... existing fields
  midiClips?: Record<string, MidiClip>;  // Optional MIDI layer
};
```

**Disadvantages:**
- Two separate data structures to keep in sync
- Complicates undo, selection, rendering
- Not recommended

---

## RENDERING IMPLICATIONS

### **Piano Roll Grid**
Must replace `drawClips()` stage:

```typescript
// For MIDI clips:
- Draw horizontal note lanes (88 or 128 note range)
- For each note event: Draw rectangle (startTime → duration, pitch → vertical position)
- Selection highlighting
- Playback cursor
```

**Coordinate transforms:**
```
pitchToY(pitch) = trackTop + trackHeight - (pitch / 128) * trackHeight
```

### **Interaction Changes**
Piano roll supports additional drag operations:
- **Drag note body** → Move startTime (quantized to grid)
- **Drag note edge** → Trim duration
- **Drag note up/down** → Change pitch
- **Draw-to-create** → Click-drag new note
- **Double-click note** → Edit velocity or other properties

---

## STORE METHOD EXTENSIONS

```typescript
// MidiClipModel-specific
addNoteEvent(clipId, note)        → Create note in clip
removeNoteEvent(clipId, noteId)   → Remove note
moveNoteEvent(clipId, noteId, newStartTime, newPitch)
trimNoteEvent(clipId, noteId, newDuration)
```

---

## UNDO COMMAND ADDITIONS

```typescript
class AddNoteEventCommand implements UndoCommand
class RemoveNoteEventCommand implements UndoCommand
class MoveNoteEventCommand implements UndoCommand  // Batch drag
```

---

## SESSION SCHEMA UPDATE

```typescript
// Flat array remains, but clipSchema becomes discriminated union:
clips: z.array(z.union([audioClipSchema, midiClipSchema]))
```

---

## KEY IMPLEMENTATION PATTERNS TO FOLLOW

1. **Immutability:** All updates via store methods, never direct mutation
2. **Referential integrity:** Maintain clip lists in tracks
3. **Undo capture:** Before/after state, deterministic reverse ops
4. **Hit testing first:** Canvas interactions depend on precise hit detection
5. **View-agnostic store:** Store doesn't know about pixels/zoom
6. **Discriminated unions:** Use TypeScript type guards, not runtime checks

This architecture is **solid and extensible** for MIDI without major refactoring.

```typescript
  readonly startTime: number;        // Timeline position (seconds)
  readonly sourceOffset: number;     // Offset into source (seconds)
  readonly duration: number;         // Clip duration (seconds)
  readonly gain: number;             // Clip volume (0..2)
  readonly fadeIn: number;           // Fade in duration (seconds)
  readonly fadeOut: number;          // Fade out duration (seconds)
  readonly name: string;             // Display name
};
```

**Key observations:**
- Currently only supports **audio clips** with media pool sources
- `sourceId` points to audio files, not applicable to MIDI
- `sourceOffset` is for seeking within audio sources
- No note/event data structure exists
- Linear/flat properties; immutable throughout

#### **TrackModel (src/state/track/types.ts)**
```typescript
type TrackModel = {
  readonly id: string;
  readonly name: string;
  readonly type: TrackType;          // "audio" | "instrument"
  readonly color: string;            // #RRGGBB format
  readonly muted: boolean;
  readonly solo: boolean;
  readonly armed: boolean;
  readonly soloIsolate: boolean;
  readonly volume: number;           // 0..2
  readonly pan: number;              // -1..1
  readonly clipIds: readonly string[];  // Child clip references
};
```

**Key observations:**

- `clipIds` array maintains order and composition of clips
- No per-track MIDI routing or instrument configuration
- Tracks are container-agnostic (could hold audio or MIDI clips)

```
removeClip(id)             → Removes clip + updates track.clipIds
moveClip(id, time, track?) → Updates startTime and/or trackId
splitClip(id, atTime)      → Creates two clips, returns right clip ID
trimClip(id, start, end)   → Adjusts startTime/duration/sourceOffset
duplicateClip(id)          → Creates copy at startTime + duration
```

**Critical detail:** When clips are moved/added, the `clipIds` array on Track is automatically synchronized. This is a **referential integrity pattern** that must be maintained for MIDI clips too.

#### **State Shape:**

input.command:
bd update long-running-harness-nl5 --description="$(cat <<'DESCEOF'
Canvas-based note grid editor (pitch vertical x time horizontal, NOT React DOM per NFR-13). Note input: click to place, drag to set duration. Note selection: click, shift-click, rubber-band lasso. Note operations: move (drag), resize (edge handles), delete (Delete key). Velocity editing: bottom lane with per-note bars. Quantization: snap notes to grid at configurable resolution (1/4, 1/8, 1/16, triplets). CC lane editing (mod wheel, expression). Zoom and scroll (independent from arrangement view). Virtual keyboard sidebar for note preview (sounds synth on click). Web MIDI API input (R-OPT-01) with validation layer: clamp note/CC to 0-127, pitch bend to 0-16383, SysEx cap 1KB, all MIDI as untrusted (NFR-16). Live MIDI recording: arm instrument track, press play, record incoming MIDI to new clip. Keyboard shortcuts for common ops (pencil, select, erase tools). EARS: R-EVT-04, R-EVT-17, R-OPT-01, NFR-04, NFR-13, NFR-16. Interface exports: PianoRollEditor, MIDINoteEvent, MIDIInputValidator. QA: note placement precision, quantization correctness, MIDI input validation.

## Acceptance Criteria
| ID | Source Req | Criterion | Verification Method |
|----|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| AC-1 | R-EVT-04 | When user clicks piano roll or virtual keyboard, synth voice allocated within 10ms | unit test + manual |
| AC-2 | R-EVT-04 | When note placed in piano roll, correct pitch and time stored in MidiClipModel | unit test |
| AC-3 | R-EVT-17 | When user presses P/S/E keys, tool switches to pencil/select/erase without mouse | unit test |
| AC-4 | R-EVT-17 | When user presses Delete key with notes selected, selected notes removed | unit test |
| AC-5 | R-OPT-01 | Where MIDI device connected, note-on/off messages route to selected instrument track | integration test |
| AC-6 | R-OPT-01 | Where MIDI device connected and track armed, live recording captures notes to new clip | integration test |
| AC-7 | NFR-04 | All piano roll controls keyboard-navigable | manual |
| AC-8 | NFR-13 | Piano roll renders to HTML5 Canvas, not React DOM elements | unit test (no DOM query for note elements) |
| AC-9 | NFR-13 | Playhead and note rendering use requestAnimationFrame loop | code review |
| AC-10 | NFR-16 | MIDI note/CC values clamped to 0-127 | unit test |
| AC-11 | NFR-16 | Pitch bend values clamped to 0-16383 | unit test |
| AC-12 | NFR-16 | SysEx messages over 1KB rejected | unit test |
| AC-13 | NFR-16 | All external MIDI input treated as untrusted (validated before use) | unit test |
| AC-14 | - | Quantization snaps to 1/4, 1/8, 1/16, and triplet grid | unit test |
| AC-15 | - | Rubber-band lasso selects all notes within rectangle | unit test |
| AC-16 | - | Note move/resize operations support undo/redo | unit test |
| AC-17 | - | Virtual keyboard sidebar sounds synth on click | manual |
| AC-18 | - | Zoom/scroll independent from arrangement view | unit test |

## Verification Contract
Profile: webapp
Surfaces:
- ui_surface
- performance_sensitive
Risks:
- user_visible_quality
- input_validation_security
Required evidence:
- test
- lint
- build
- runtime_startup
- edge_states_check
- console_network_clean
- performance_budget_check
DESCEOF
)" 2>&1

Epic: long-running-harness-nl5 - E11: Piano Roll & MIDI Editor. Execute all 5 implementation tasks in dependency order: T1 (data model) -> T2 (renderer) + T4 (MIDI validation) in parallel -> T3 (interactions) -> T5 (panel component).

# Work

**MANDATORY FIRST STEP -- NON-NEGOTIABLE**: Use the Read tool to open and read `.claude/skills/compound/work/SKILL.md` NOW. Do NOT proceed until you have read the complete skill file. It contains the full workflow you must follow.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: Work
3	description: Team-based TDD execution with adaptive complexity and agent delegation
4	---
5	
6	# Work Skill
7	
8	## Overview
9	Execute implementation through an AgentTeam using adaptive TDD. The lead coordinates and delegates -- agents write code.
10	
11	## Methodology
12	1. Pick tasks from `bd ready` or `$ARGUMENTS`
13	2. Mark tasks in progress: `bd update <id> --status=in_progress`
14	3. Read the epic description (`bd show <epic>`) for spec context -- EARS requirements guide what "done" looks like
15	4. **Read Acceptance Criteria**: Locate the `## Acceptance Criteria` table in the parent epic description. Each AC row defines a testable criterion that the implementation must satisfy. If no AC section exists, flag as a process gap and proceed using EARS requirements directly.
16	5. **Read Verification Contract**: Locate the `## Verification Contract` section in the parent epic description.
17	   - Treat `Required evidence` as the epic's definition of done.
18	   - Use `Profile`, `Surfaces`, and `Risks` to decide what evidence must be produced during implementation, not just at review time.
19	   - If the contract is **missing**: flag it as a process gap and fall back to the acceptance criteria, EARS requirements, and legacy `test` + `lint` gates.
20	6. Run `ca search` per agent/subtask for targeted context. Display results.
21	7. Assess parallelization: identify independent tasks that can be worked simultaneously
22	8. Deploy an **AgentTeam** (TeamCreate + Task with `team_name`) with MULTIPLE test-writers and implementers:
23	   - Role skills: `.claude/skills/compound/agents/{test-writer,implementer}/SKILL.md`
24	   - Scale teammate count to independent tasks; pairs coordinate via SendMessage on shared interfaces
25	9. Agents communicate via SendMessage when working on overlapping areas.
26	10. Lead coordinates: review agent outputs, resolve conflicts, verify tests pass. Do not write code directly.
27	11. If implementation diverges from spec requirements, acceptance criteria, or the Verification Contract, stop and discuss with user via AskUserQuestion before proceeding.
28	12. If blocked, use AskUserQuestion to get user direction.
29	13. Shut down the team when done: send shutdown_request to all teammates.
30	14. Commit incrementally as tests pass.
31	15. Run full test suite for regressions.
32	16. Close tasks: `bd close <id>`
33	
34	## Memory Integration
35	- Run `ca search` per delegated subtask with the subtask's specific description
36	- Each agent receives memory items tailored to their assigned task, not a shared blob
37	- Run `ca learn` after corrections or novel discoveries
38	
39	## MANDATORY VERIFICATION -- DO NOT CLOSE TASK WITHOUT THIS
40	Before `bd close`, you MUST:
41	1. Read the parent epic's `Required evidence` list and satisfy every listed item. The minimum legacy floor is `test` and `lint`.
42	2. Run `pnpm test` then `pnpm lint`
43	3. If `Required evidence` includes `build`, run `pnpm build`
44	4. Produce the contract-specific evidence needed for the touched surfaces:
45	   - `browser_evidence` / `responsive_check` / `edge_states_check` / `a11y_smoke`: capture runtime/browser proof and summarize it in the review handoff
46	   - `contract_checks` / `contract_examples`: run representative requests/examples and record outcomes
47	   - `command_transcript`: record happy-path and bad-input CLI flows
48	   - `examples_run` / `docs_examples_sync`: run or update public examples alongside the code
49	5. Run `/implementation-reviewer` on changed code -- wait for APPROVED
50	If REJECTED: fix ALL issues, re-run required evidence, resubmit. INVIOLABLE per CLAUDE.md.
51	
52	The full 8-step pipeline (invariant-designer through implementation-reviewer) is recommended
53	for complex changes. For all changes, `/implementation-reviewer` is the minimum required gate.
54	
55	## Beads Lifecycle
56	- `bd ready` to find available tasks
57	- `bd update <id> --status=in_progress` when starting
58	- `bd close <id>` when all tests pass
59	
60	## Parallelization Strategy
61	- **Always prefer parallel work**: independent tasks should be assigned to different teammate pairs simultaneously
62	- **Scale the team adaptively**: deploy multiple test-writer + implementer pairs proportional to independent task count
63	- **Subagent spawning within teammates**: each teammate should spawn opus subagents for independent subtasks (e.g., a test-writer spawning subagents to write tests for multiple modules in parallel)
64	- **Coordinate on shared interfaces**: teammates working on overlapping APIs must communicate via SendMessage before implementing
65	
66	## Literature
67	- Consult `docs/compound/research/tdd/` for TDD methodology, test-first development evidence, and best practices

file.content:
---
name: Work
description: Team-based TDD execution with adaptive complexity and agent delegation
---

# Work Skill

## Overview
Execute implementation through an AgentTeam using adaptive TDD. The lead coordinates and delegates -- agents write code.

## Methodology
1. Pick tasks from `bd ready` or `$ARGUMENTS`
2. Mark tasks in progress: `bd update <id> --status=in_progress`
3. Read the epic description (`bd show <epic>`) for spec context -- EARS requirements guide what "done" looks like
4. **Read Acceptance Criteria**: Locate the `## Acceptance Criteria` table in the parent epic description. Each AC row defines a testable criterion that the implementation must satisfy. If no AC section exists, flag as a process gap and proceed using EARS requirements directly.
5. **Read Verification Contract**: Locate the `## Verification Contract` section in the parent epic description.
   - Treat `Required evidence` as the epic's definition of done.
   - Use `Profile`, `Surfaces`, and `Risks` to decide what evidence must be produced during implementation, not just at review time.
   - If the contract is **missing**: flag it as a process gap and fall back to the acceptance criteria, EARS requirements, and legacy `test` + `lint` gates.
6. Run `ca search` per agent/subtask for targeted context. Display results.
7. Assess parallelization: identify independent tasks that can be worked simultaneously
8. Deploy an **AgentTeam** (TeamCreate + Task with `team_name`) with MULTIPLE test-writers and implementers:
   - Role skills: `.claude/skills/compound/agents/{test-writer,implementer}/SKILL.md`
   - Scale teammate count to independent tasks; pairs coordinate via SendMessage on shared interfaces
9. Agents communicate via SendMessage when working on overlapping areas.
10. Lead coordinates: review agent outputs, resolve conflicts, verify tests pass. Do not write code directly.
11. If implementation diverges from spec requirements, acceptance criteria, or the Verification Contract, stop and discuss with user via AskUserQuestion before proceeding.
12. If blocked, use AskUserQuestion to get user direction.
13. Shut down the team when done: send shutdown_request to all teammates.
14. Commit incrementally as tests pass.
15. Run full test suite for regressions.
16. Close tasks: `bd close <id>`

## Memory Integration
- Run `ca search` per delegated subtask with the subtask's specific description
- Each agent receives memory items tailored to their assigned task, not a shared blob
- Run `ca learn` after corrections or novel discoveries

## MANDATORY VERIFICATION -- DO NOT CLOSE TASK WITHOUT THIS
Before `bd close`, you MUST:
1. Read the parent epic's `Required evidence` list and satisfy every listed item. The minimum legacy floor is `test` and `lint`.
2. Run `pnpm test` then `pnpm lint`
3. If `Required evidence` includes `build`, run `pnpm build`
4. Produce the contract-specific evidence needed for the touched surfaces:
   - `browser_evidence` / `responsive_check` / `edge_states_check` / `a11y_smoke`: capture runtime/browser proof and summarize it in the review handoff
   - `contract_checks` / `contract_examples`: run representative requests/examples and record outcomes
   - `command_transcript`: record happy-path and bad-input CLI flows
   - `examples_run` / `docs_examples_sync`: run or update public examples alongside the code
5. Run `/implementation-reviewer` on changed code -- wait for APPROVED
If REJECTED: fix ALL issues, re-run required evidence, resubmit. INVIOLABLE per CLAUDE.md.

The full 8-step pipeline (invariant-designer through implementation-reviewer) is recommended
for complex changes. For all changes, `/implementation-reviewer` is the minimum required gate.

## Beads Lifecycle
- `bd ready` to find available tasks
- `bd update <id> --status=in_progress` when starting
- `bd close <id>` when all tests pass

## Parallelization Strategy
- **Always prefer parallel work**: independent tasks should be assigned to different teammate pairs simultaneously
- **Scale the team adaptively**: deploy multiple test-writer + implementer pairs proportional to independent task count
- **Subagent spawning within teammates**: each teammate should spawn opus subagents for independent subtasks (e.g., a test-writer spawning subagents to write tests for multiple modules in parallel)
- **Coordinate on shared interfaces**: teammates working on overlapping APIs must communicate via SendMessage before implementing

## Literature
- Consult `docs/compound/research/tdd/` for TDD methodology, test-first development evidence, and best practices
- Consult `docs/compound/research/property-testing/` for property-based testing theory and invariant design
- Run `ca knowledge "TDD test-first"` for indexed knowledge on testing methodology
- Run `ca search "testing"` for lessons from past TDD cycles

## Common Pitfalls
- Lead writing code instead of delegating to agents
- Not injecting memory context into agent prompts
- Modifying tests to make them pass instead of fixing implementation
- Not running the full test suite after agent work completes
- Ignoring acceptance criteria from the parent epic
- Ignoring the Verification Contract and closing work with only test/lint evidence

## Quality Criteria
- Tests existed before implementation code
- Agents received relevant memory context
- Lead coordinated without writing implementation code
- Incremental commits made as tests pass
- All tests pass after refactoring
- Task lifecycle tracked via beads (`bd`)
- Implementation aligns with spec requirements from epic
- **Implementation satisfies acceptance criteria from parent epic**
- **Implementation produced the evidence required by the parent epic's Verification Contract**

## PHASE GATE 3 -- MANDATORY
Before starting Review, verify ALL work tasks are closed:
- `bd list --status=in_progress` must return empty
- `bd list --status=open` should only have Review and Compound tasks remaining
If any work tasks remain open, DO NOT proceed. Complete them first.

input.description: T1: Write tests for MIDI model
input.prompt:
You are implementing E11-T1 for a DAW project: MIDI note data model, store extensions, and undo commands.

## Context

The existing codebase has:
- `src/state/track/types.ts` with TrackModel (type: "audio" | "instrument") and ClipModel (audio-only, has sourceId, sourceOffset, gain, fadeIn, fadeOut)
- `src/state/store.ts` with a Zustand store (useDawStore) that manages tracks and clips
- `src/state/track/track-commands.ts` with undo commands (AddClipCommand, RemoveClipCommand, MoveClipCommand, etc.)
- `src/state/track/track-schema.ts` with Zod schemas for validation
- `src/state/session/session-schema.ts` with session persistence

## Your Task - Write Tests FIRST (TDD Red Phase)

Write comprehensive test files for the MIDI note data model. The tests should cover:

### File 1: `src/state/track/midi-types.test.ts`
Test the MIDINoteEvent type and MidiClipModel:
- MIDINoteEvent has: id, pitch (0-127), velocity (0-127), startTime (seconds, relative to clip start), duration (seconds)
- ClipModel becomes a discriminated union: AudioClipModel (type: "audio") | MidiClipModel (type: "midi")
- MidiClipModel has: id, trackId, type: "midi", startTime, duration, noteEvents (readonly MIDINoteEvent[]), name
- AudioClipModel has all original ClipModel fields + type: "audio"
- Type guard functions: isAudioClip(), isMidiClip()

### File 2: `src/state/track/midi-store.test.ts`
Test store extensions for MIDI clip/note management:
- addMidiClip(clip: MidiClipModel) - adds a MIDI clip to store and track
- removeMidiClip(id) - removes MIDI clip
- addNoteEvent(clipId, note: MIDINoteEvent) - adds note to a MIDI clip
- removeNoteEvent(clipId, noteId) - removes note from MIDI clip
- moveNoteEvent(clipId, noteId, newStartTime, newPitch) - moves a note
- resizeNoteEvent(clipId, noteId, newDuration) - resizes a note
- updateNoteVelocity(clipId, noteId, velocity) - updates velocity
- setSelectedNoteIds(ids) - tracks note selection
- The store clips Record should accept both ClipModel types (AudioClipModel | MidiClipModel)

### File 3: `src/state/track/midi-commands.test.ts`
Test undo commands for note operations:
- AddNoteCommand - adds note, undo removes it
- RemoveNoteCommand - removes note, undo restores it
- MoveNoteCommand - moves note, undo restores original position/pitch
- ResizeNoteCommand - resizes note, undo restores original duration
- BatchNoteCommand - batches multiple note operations (for multi-select move/resize)

### File 4: `src/state/track/midi-schema.test.ts`
Test Zod schemas:
- noteEventSchema validates MIDINoteEvent (pitch 0-127, velocity 0-127, positive startTime, positive duration)
- midiClipSchema validates MidiClipModel
- audioClipSchema validates AudioClipModel
- clipSchema validates discriminated union (both types)
- Rejects invalid pitch (negative, >127, non-integer)
- Rejects invalid velocity (negative, >127)
- Session schema accepts MIDI clips

## Test Infrastructure

Use Vitest (globals: true). Tests are in the same directory as source. Import pattern:
```typescript
import { describe, it, expect, beforeEach } from "vitest";
import { useDawStore } from "@state/store";
```

For store tests, reset store between tests:
```typescript
beforeEach(() => {
  useDawStore.setState({
    tracks: [],
    clips: {},
    selectedTrackIds: [],
    selectedClipIds: [],
    // ... reset relevant state
  });
});
```

Import the types/functions that WILL BE created (they don't exist yet - tests should fail):
```typescript
import type { MIDINoteEvent, MidiClipModel, AudioClipModel } from "@state/track/types";
import { isAudioClip, isMidiClip } from "@state/track/types";
import { AddNoteCommand, RemoveNoteCommand, MoveNoteCommand, ResizeNoteCommand, BatchNoteCommand } from "@state/track/midi-commands";
import { noteEventSchema, midiClipSchema, audioClipSchema } from "@state/track/track-schema";
```

Write all 4 test files now. Make them comprehensive but lean - test behavior, not implementation details.

All four test files fail for the expected reasons:

1. **midi-types.test.ts**: 10 pass (type structure tests), 4 fail because `isAudioClip`/`isMidiClip` functions don't exist yet.
2. **midi-store.test.ts**: All 16 fail because `addMidiClip`, `removeMidiClip`, `addNoteEvent`, `removeNoteEvent`, `moveNoteEvent`, `resizeNoteEvent`, `updateNoteVelocity`, and `setSelectedNoteIds` don't exist on the store yet.
3. **midi-commands.test.ts**: Fails at import because `./midi-commands` module doesn't exist yet.
4. **midi-schema.test.ts**: 3 pass (existing clipSchema tests), 24 fail because `noteEventSchema`, `midiClipSchema`, `audioClipSchema` don't exist yet, and `clipSchema` is not yet a discriminated union.

All failures are due to missing implementations, not test bugs. This is the correct TDD Red state.

---

Here is a summary of the four test files created:

**`/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/state/track/midi-types.test.ts`** (14 tests)
- Tests `MIDINoteEvent` structure (pitch, velocity, startTime, duration, boundary values)
- Tests `MidiClipModel` structure (type discriminator, noteEvents array, common fields)
- Tests `AudioClipModel` structure (type discriminator, audio-specific fields)
- Tests `ClipModel` discriminated union (accepts both types)
- Tests `isAudioClip()` and `isMidiClip()` type guards

**`/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/state/track/midi-store.test.ts`** (16 tests)
- Tests `addMidiClip` (adds to clips map, wires clipIds, preserves noteEvents)
- Tests `removeMidiClip` (removes from clips map and track)
- Tests mixed clip storage (audio + midi side by side)
- Tests `addNoteEvent`, `removeNoteEvent`, `moveNoteEvent`, `resizeNoteEvent`, `updateNoteVelocity` (each with behavior + field preservation)
- Tests `setSelectedNoteIds` (set and clear)

**`/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/state/track/midi-commands.test.ts`** (25 tests)
- Tests `AddNoteCommand` (execute, undo, type, serialize)
- Tests `RemoveNoteCommand` (execute, undo restores note, type, serialize)
- Tests `MoveNoteCommand` (execute, undo restores position/pitch, preserves other fields, type, serialize)
- Tests `ResizeNoteCommand` (execute, undo restores duration, preserves other fields, type, serialize)
- Tests `BatchNoteCommand` (executes all sub-commands, undo reverses in order, works with mixed types, type, serialize)

**`/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/src/state/track/midi-schema.test.ts`** (27 tests)
- Tests `noteEventSchema` (valid data, pitch 0-127 int-only, velocity 0-127, startTime >= 0, duration > 0)
- Tests `midiClipSchema` (valid MIDI clip, empty noteEvents, rejects wrong type, rejects invalid nested notes)
- Tests `audioClipSchema` (valid audio clip, rejects wrong type, rejects invalid gain/sourceOffset)
- Tests `clipSchema` as discriminated union (accepts both types, rejects unknown type, rejects missing discriminator-specific fields)

tool_use_result.prompt:
You are implementing E11-T1 for a DAW project: MIDI note data model, store extensions, and undo commands.

## Context

The existing codebase has:
- `src/state/track/types.ts` with TrackModel (type: "audio" | "instrument") and ClipModel (audio-only, has sourceId, sourceOffset, gain, fadeIn, fadeOut)
- `src/state/store.ts` with a Zustand store (useDawStore) that manages tracks and clips
- `src/state/track/track-commands.ts` with undo commands (AddClipCommand, RemoveClipCommand, MoveClipCommand, etc.)
- `src/state/track/track-schema.ts` with Zod schemas for validation
- `src/state/session/session-schema.ts` with session persistence

## Your Task - Write Tests FIRST (TDD Red Phase)

Write comprehensive test files for the MIDI note data model. The tests should cover:

### File 1: `src/state/track/midi-types.test.ts`
Test the MIDINoteEvent type and MidiClipModel:
- MIDINoteEvent has: id, pitch (0-127), velocity (0-127), startTime (seconds, relative to clip start), duration (seconds)
- ClipModel becomes a discriminated union: AudioClipModel (type: "audio") | MidiClipModel (type: "midi")
- MidiClipModel has: id, trackId, type: "midi", startTime, duration, noteEvents (readonly MIDINoteEvent[]), name
- AudioClipModel has all original ClipModel fields + type: "audio"
- Type guard functions: isAudioClip(), isMidiClip()

### File 2: `src/state/track/midi-store.test.ts`
Test store extensions for MIDI clip/note management:
- addMidiClip(clip: MidiClipModel) - adds a MIDI clip to store and track
- removeMidiClip(id) - removes MIDI clip
- addNoteEvent(clipId, note: MIDINoteEvent) - adds note to a MIDI clip
- removeNoteEvent(clipId, noteId) - removes note from MIDI clip
- moveNoteEvent(clipId, noteId, newStartTime, newPitch) - moves a note
- resizeNoteEvent(clipId, noteId, newDuration) - resizes a note
- updateNoteVelocity(clipId, noteId, velocity) - updates velocity
- setSelectedNoteIds(ids) - tracks note selection
- The store clips Record should accept both ClipModel types (AudioClipModel | MidiClipModel)

### File 3: `src/state/track/midi-commands.test.ts`
Test undo commands for note operations:
- AddNoteCommand - adds note, undo removes it
- RemoveNoteCommand - removes note, undo restores it
- MoveNoteCommand - moves note, undo restores original position/pitch
- ResizeNoteCommand - resizes note, undo restores original duration
- BatchNoteCommand - batches multiple note operations (for multi-select move/resize)

### File 4: `src/state/track/midi-schema.test.ts`
Test Zod schemas:
- noteEventSchema validates MIDINoteEvent (pitch 0-127, velocity 0-127, positive startTime, positive duration)
- midiClipSchema validates MidiClipModel
- audioClipSchema validates AudioClipModel
- clipSchema validates discriminated union (both types)
- Rejects invalid pitch (negative, >127, non-integer)
- Rejects invalid velocity (negative, >127)
- Session schema accepts MIDI clips

## Test Infrastructure

Use Vitest (globals: true). Tests are in the same directory as source. Import pattern:
```typescript
import { describe, it, expect, beforeEach } from "vitest";
import { useDawStore } from "@state/store";
```

For store tests, reset store between tests:
```typescript
beforeEach(() => {
  useDawStore.setState({
    tracks: [],
    clips: {},
    selectedTrackIds: [],
    selectedClipIds: [],
    // ... reset relevant state
  });
});
```

Import the types/functions that WILL BE created (they don't exist yet - tests should fail):
```typescript
import type { MIDINoteEvent, MidiClipModel, AudioClipModel } from "@state/track/types";
import { isAudioClip, isMidiClip } from "@state/track/types";
import { AddNoteCommand, RemoveNoteCommand, MoveNoteCommand, ResizeNoteCommand, BatchNoteCommand } from "@state/track/midi-commands";
import { noteEventSchema, midiClipSchema, audioClipSchema } from "@state/track/track-schema";
```

Write all 4 test files now. Make them comprehensive but lean - test behavior, not implementation details.

content.text:
All 940 tests pass, 0 failures, lint clean.

---

Here is a summary of what was implemented:

## Files Created

**`src/audio/midi/midi-input-validator.ts`** -- MIDI message validation and clamping per NFR-16:
- `clampMidi7Bit()` / `clampMidi14Bit()` -- range-clamping utility functions
- `validateMidiMessage()` -- parses raw `Uint8Array` from Web MIDI API into typed, validated messages (note-on, note-off, CC, pitch bend). Converts note-on with velocity 0 to note-off. Rejects unknown status bytes.
- `validateSysEx()` -- validates SysEx framing (F0/F7) and enforces 1KB cap
- Safe byte accessor (`byte()` helper) to avoid non-null assertions on indexed access

**`src/audio/midi/midi-device-manager.ts`** -- Web MIDI API integration per R-OPT-01:
- `createMidiDeviceManager()` factory returning a `MidiDeviceManager` object
- Handles `requestMIDIAccess`, device enumeration, connect/disconnect, message routing through `validateMidiMessage`, and cleanup via `dispose()`

**`src/audio/midi/midi-recorder.ts`** -- Live MIDI recording with open-note-map pattern:
- `createMidiRecorder()` factory with `idle -> armed -> recording` state machine
- Pairs note-on/note-off into `MIDINoteEvent` objects with computed relative `startTime` and `duration`
- Auto-closes open notes on `stopRecording()`

**`src/audio/midi/index.ts`** -- Barrel exports for all types and functions

**Test files** (written first per TDD):
- `midi-input-validator.test.ts` -- 18 tests covering clamping, all message types, out-of-range handling, edge cases, SysEx validation
- `midi-recorder.test.ts` -- 12 tests covering state machine, note pairing, duration computation, simultaneous notes, open note closure
- `midi-device-manager.test.ts` -- 7 tests covering creation, graceful degradation without Web MIDI API, lifecycle methods

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
E11-T2/T4: Add piano roll renderer and MIDI input validation

T2 - Piano roll canvas renderer:
- Pure canvas renderer following arrangement-renderer.ts pattern
- 8 render stages: background, note grid, time grid, notes, keyboard, velocity lane, playhead, ruler
- Helper functions: secondsToX, pitchToY, isBlackKey, noteName
- Neo-brutalist styling with design tokens

T4 - MIDI input validation and Web MIDI API:
- MIDIInputValidator: clamp note/CC to 0-127, pitch bend to 0-16383, SysEx cap 1KB
- Web MIDI API device manager with connection lifecycle
- Live MIDI recorder with openNoteMap pattern for note-on/off pairing
- All external MIDI input treated as untrusted per NFR-16

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text:
Clean -- zero errors, only the pre-existing warning. Here is a summary of what was done.

---

## Summary

Implemented E11-T5: PianoRollEditor panel component and keyboard shortcuts following TDD.

### Files created

1. **`src/ui/piano-roll/use-piano-roll-interactions.ts`** -- Interaction hook handling all pointer events:
   - **Pencil tool**: Creates notes at snapped grid positions on empty-grid click, supports draw-drag to extend duration
   - **Select tool**: Click to select notes, shift-click to toggle, drag note body to move, drag edges to resize, rubber-band lasso on empty grid
   - **Erase tool**: Deletes notes on click
   - **deleteSelectedNotes()**: Batch-removes all selected notes
   - All mutations push appropriate undo commands (`AddNoteCommand`, `RemoveNoteCommand`, `MoveNoteCommand`, `ResizeNoteCommand`, `BatchNoteCommand`)
   - Cursor changes based on tool and hit-test result

2. **`src/ui/piano-roll/PianoRollEditor.tsx`** -- Main React component following the ArrangementPanel pattern:
   - Canvas ref + ResizeObserver for DPI-aware sizing
   - RAF loop for rendering (continuous during playback, triggered on state change otherwise)
   - Toolbar with Pencil/Select/Erase buttons and grid snap dropdown
   - Keyboard shortcuts: P (pencil), S (select), E (erase), Delete/Backspace (delete selected)
   - Wheel handler for zoom (ctrl+wheel) and scroll
   - Zustand store selectors for reactive state

3. **`src/ui/piano-roll/PianoRollEditor.module.css`** -- CSS Module using design tokens from `tokens.css`

4. **`src/ui/piano-roll/index.ts`** -- Barrel exports for the piano-roll module

### Test files created

5. **`src/ui/piano-roll/use-piano-roll-interactions.test.ts`** -- 12 tests covering pencil create, erase delete, select/shift-select/deselect, cursor changes, move-note drag, resize-note drag, delete-selected, and null-clip handling

6. **`src/ui/piano-roll/PianoRollEditor.test.tsx`** -- 7 tests covering rendering, toolbar buttons, active state, tool switching, grid snap selector, and canvas element presence

### Results
- All 981 tests pass (95 test files)
- Lint: 0 errors (only 1 pre-existing warning in unrelated file)
- TypeScript strict mode: compiles cleanly

content.text:
Epic: long-running-harness-nl5 - E11: Piano Roll & MIDI Editor. Review all implementation code for the 5 tasks: T1 (MIDI data model, store, undo), T2 (canvas renderer), T3 (hit testing, interactions, quantization), T4 (MIDI input validation, Web MIDI API), T5 (PianoRollEditor panel component).

# Review

**MANDATORY FIRST STEP -- NON-NEGOTIABLE**: Use the Read tool to open and read `.claude/skills/compound/review/SKILL.md` NOW. Do NOT proceed until you have read the complete skill file. It contains the full workflow you must follow.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: Review
3	description: Multi-agent review with lesson-calibrated reviewers, runtime verification, and severity classification
4	---
5	
6	# Review Skill
7	
8	## Overview
9	Perform thorough code review by spawning specialized reviewers in parallel, consolidating findings with severity classification (P0/P1/P2/P3), and gating completion on implementation-reviewer approval. Reviewers are calibrated with past lessons and relevant research before reviewing.
10	
11	## Methodology
12	1. Read the epic description (`bd show <epic>`) for EARS requirements -- reviewers verify each requirement is met
13	2. **Check Acceptance Criteria**: Locate the `## Acceptance Criteria` table in the epic description. For each AC row, verify the implementation satisfies the criterion using the specified verification method.
14	   - If the AC section is **missing**: flag as **P1 process finding** ("No Acceptance Criteria section found in epic description — plan phase did not generate AC table")
15	   - If an AC criterion is **not met**: flag as **P1 defect** ("AC-N not satisfied: <details>")
16	   - If an AC criterion is **met**: annotate the AC row as PASS in the review report
17	3. **Check Verification Contract**: Locate the `## Verification Contract` section in the epic description.
18	   - If the contract is **missing**: flag as **P1 process finding** ("No Verification Contract section found in epic description — plan phase did not define required evidence")
19	   - If the contract exists: verify each item in `Required evidence` explicitly. Missing evidence is a **P1 process or execution defect**, not an optional note.
20	   - Use `Profile`, `Surfaces`, and `Risks` to decide which verification specialists must run during this review.
21	4. Run baseline quality gates: `pnpm test` and `pnpm lint`
22	   - If `Required evidence` includes `build`, also run `pnpm build` before spawning reviewers
23	5. **Lesson-Calibrated Review (LCR)**:
24	   a. For each reviewer category (security, test-coverage, simplicity, architecture, etc.), run `ca search "<category> review"` to retrieve relevant past lessons
25	   b. Filter results by category match and recency (prefer lessons < 30 days old)
26	   c. Cap at **3-5 lessons per reviewer** -- more dilutes focus
27	   d. Inject matched lessons into each reviewer's prompt as calibration context
28	   e. Consult `docs/compound/research/` for methodology references relevant to the review domain (e.g., `docs/compound/research/scenario-testing/` for testing reviews)
29	   f. **Contradiction detection**: If a reviewer finding contradicts a high-severity lesson (severity >= P1), flag the contradiction for human review via `AskUserQuestion` with both the finding and the lesson content. Do not auto-resolve contradictions.
30	   > See `review/references/lesson-calibration.md` for detailed calibration guidance
31	6. Search memory with `ca search` for known patterns and recurring issues (broader search beyond per-reviewer calibration)
32	7. Select reviewer tier based on diff size:
33	   - **Small** (<100 lines): 4 core -- security, test-coverage, simplicity, cct-subagent
34	   - **Medium** (100-500): add architecture, performance, scenario-coverage, pattern-matcher (8 total)
35	   - **Large** (500+): all reviewers including doc-gardener, drift-detector, runtime-verifier
36	8. **Runtime Verification (contract-driven)**: Spawn `runtime-verifier` when the Verification Contract requires runtime proof.
37	   - If `Required evidence` includes `runtime_startup`, `browser_evidence`, or `contract_checks`, spawn `runtime-verifier`
38	   - **Web UI project**: runtime-verifier validates startup and browser-level behavior
39	   - **HTTP API project**: runtime-verifier validates startup and representative request/response behavior
40	   - **CLI/library/service without runtime evidence in the contract**: **SKIP** runtime-verifier and record INFO ("Runtime verification not required by Verification Contract")
41	   - Role skill: `.claude/skills/compound/agents/runtime-verifier/SKILL.md`
42	   - Timeout: 5min total suite, 2min per individual test
43	   > See RV-1 through RV-5 in epic for full requirements
44	9. **QA Engineer (required when contract asks for browser/UI evidence)**:
45	   - If `Required evidence` includes `browser_evidence`, `responsive_check`, `edge_states_check`, `console_network_clean`, or `a11y_smoke`, invoke the QA Engineer skill and treat its findings as part of the blocking review result
46	   - Otherwise, use QA Engineer optionally when reviewers request hands-on verification
47	   - Skill: `.claude/skills/compound/qa-engineer/SKILL.md`
48	   - Primary triggers: contract-required browser/UI evidence, visual changes (CSS, HTML, component files), new pages/routes, form modifications, accessibility-related changes
49	   - The QA Engineer complements runtime-verifier (step 8): runtime-verifier covers automated contract tests, QA Engineer covers exploratory/visual/boundary testing
50	   - When the Verification Contract requires browser/UI evidence, this step is **not optional**
51	   - Can also run between review cycles in the infinity loop (see `review-fleet.md`)
52	10. **Design Craft Review (contract-driven)**: Spawn `design-craft-reviewer` when the Verification Contract requires design quality proof.
53	   - If `Required evidence` includes `design_craft_check`, spawn `design-craft-reviewer`
54	   - The reviewer evaluates changed UI code against the `build-great-things` quality checklist: states (loading/empty/error), interaction feedback (hover/active/focus/transitions), visual craft (spacing scale, typography hierarchy, color semantics, shadows, borders), motion, responsiveness, and accessibility
55	   - **Non-UI projects without `design_craft_check` in the contract**: **SKIP** and record INFO ("Design craft review not required by Verification Contract")
56	   - Role skill: `.claude/skills/compound/agents/design-craft-reviewer/SKILL.md`
57	10b. **Visual Verification (contract-driven)**: When the Verification Contract includes `browser_evidence`, `design_craft_check`, or `responsive_check`, take Playwright screenshots as evidence.
58	   - Auto-detect: Use the QA Engineer detection priority (see `.claude/skills/compound/qa-engineer/SKILL.md` Phase 1) to check for a runnable UI. If no UI framework detected, **SKIP** and record INFO.
59	   - **If UI detected**: Start the dev server in the background, wait for HTTP readiness (poll every 1s, timeout 30s), then take headless Playwright screenshots at 4 viewports: 375px (mobile), 768px (tablet), 1024px (small desktop), 1440px (desktop). Navigate key routes (up to 10) and screenshot each.
60	   - **Evidence**: Save screenshots alongside review artifacts. Reference them in the Verification Contract evidence table as proof for `browser_evidence` or `responsive_check`.
61	   - **Visual critique**: Include layout, spacing, contrast, hierarchy, and responsive observations in the review findings with screenshot references.
62	   - **Graceful degradation**: If Playwright is unavailable, record a **P3/INFO** finding ("Playwright not available for visual verification") and proceed with code-only review. Tag visual concerns with [NEEDS_QA] for the QA Engineer.
63	   - **Cleanup**: Stop the dev server after screenshot capture.
64	11. Spawn reviewers in an **AgentTeam** (TeamCreate + Task with `team_name`):
65	   - Role skills: `.claude/skills/compound/agents/{security-reviewer,architecture-reviewer,performance-reviewer,test-coverage-reviewer,simplicity-reviewer,scenario-coverage-reviewer}/SKILL.md`
66	   - Security specialist skills (on-demand, spawned by security-reviewer): `.claude/skills/compound/agents/{security-injection,security-secrets,security-auth,security-data,security-deps}/SKILL.md`
67	   - Runtime verifier (conditional, see step 8): `.claude/skills/compound/agents/runtime-verifier/SKILL.md`
68	   - Design craft reviewer (conditional, see step 10): `.claude/skills/compound/agents/design-craft-reviewer/SKILL.md`
69	   - For large diffs (500+), deploy MULTIPLE instances; split files across instances, coordinate via SendMessage
70	12. Reviewers communicate findings to each other via `SendMessage`
71	13. Collect, consolidate, and deduplicate all findings (including QA Engineer findings from step 9 and design craft findings from step 10)
72	14. Classify by severity: P0 (blocks merge), P1 (critical/blocking), P2 (important), P3 (minor)
73	15. Use `AskUserQuestion` when severity is ambiguous or fix has multiple valid options
74	16. Create beads issues for P1 findings: `bd create --title="P1: ..."`
75	17. Verify spec alignment: flag unmet EARS requirements as P1, flag requirements met but missing from acceptance criteria as gaps
76	18. Fix all P1 findings before proceeding
77	19. Run `/implementation-reviewer` as mandatory gate
78	20. Capture novel findings with `ca learn`; pattern-matcher auto-reinforces recurring issues
79	
80	## Acceptance Criteria Review Protocol
81	When checking AC, produce a summary table in the review report:
82	
83	| AC ID | Criterion | Status | Evidence |
84	|-------|-----------|--------|----------|
85	| AC-1  | When X... | PASS/FAIL | test file, line N / manual check |
86	
87	All AC rows must be PASS for the review to proceed to `/implementation-reviewer`.
88	
89	## Verification Contract Review Protocol
90	When checking the Verification Contract, produce a second summary table in the review report:
91	
92	| Evidence | Status | Proof | Notes |
93	|----------|--------|-------|-------|
94	| build | PASS/FAIL | command output / artifact | optional detail |
95	
96	Rules:
97	- Every item in `Required evidence` must be marked PASS, FAIL, or ESCALATED
98	- `ESCALATED` means review strengthened the contract because the planned evidence was too weak for the actual risk
99	- Missing runtime/browser/UI evidence is a blocking review outcome, not a polish suggestion
100	- If the contract is absent, record a P1 process finding and review against the legacy floor (`test`, `lint`) plus obvious risk-based evidence
101	
102	## Lesson-Calibrated Review Protocol
103	Reviewers are pre-loaded with relevant lessons before they begin reviewing:
104	
105	### Per-Reviewer Calibration
106	Each reviewer receives lessons filtered by their domain:
107	- **security-reviewer**: `ca search "security vulnerability injection XSS"`
108	- **test-coverage-reviewer**: `ca search "testing coverage TDD mock"`
109	- **simplicity-reviewer**: `ca search "complexity refactor simplify"`
110	- **architecture-reviewer**: `ca search "architecture module boundary coupling"`
111	- **performance-reviewer**: `ca search "performance optimization latency"`
112	- **design-craft-reviewer**: `ca search "design craft visual hierarchy spacing motion states"`
113	- **pattern-matcher**: `ca search "pattern recurring mistake"`
114	
115	### Calibration Rules
116	- **Cap**: 3-5 lessons per reviewer (prevents context dilution)
117	- **Recency bias**: Prefer lessons from the last 30 days; older lessons included only if severity >= P1
118	- **Category filter**: Match lesson tags to reviewer domain; discard unrelated results
119	- **Research supplement**: Each reviewer may also receive relevant research excerpts from `docs/research/`
120	
121	### Contradiction Handling (LCR-3)
122	When a reviewer produces a finding that directly contradicts a calibration lesson:
123	1. Flag the contradiction with both the finding and the lesson text
124	2. Escalate via `AskUserQuestion` -- do NOT auto-resolve
125	3. Record resolution via `ca learn` to prevent future contradictions
126	
127	## Memory Integration
128	- Run `ca search` before review for known recurring issues
129	- **LCR**: Per-reviewer calibration queries (see Lesson-Calibrated Review Protocol above)
130	- **pattern-matcher** auto-reinforces: recurring findings get severity increased via `ca learn`
131	- **cct-subagent** reads CCT patterns for known Claude failure patterns
132	- Capture the review report via `ca learn` with `type=solution`
133	
134	## Runtime Verification Integration
135	When the runtime-verifier is triggered by the Verification Contract:
136	- Verifier generates ephemeral Playwright tests against a running application instance
137	- Findings are reported in standard P0-P3 format and merged with other reviewer findings
138	- If the app cannot be started: report as **P1/INFRA** with diagnostics (not silent skip)
139	- If no runtime target exists for the required evidence: report as **P1/INFRA** or **P3/INFO SKIPPED** with justification

148	- Consult `docs/compound/research/code-review/` for systematic review methodology, severity taxonomies, and evidence-based review practices
149	- Run `ca knowledge "code review methodology"` for indexed knowledge on review techniques
150	- Run `ca search "review"` for lessons from past review cycles
151	
152	## Common Pitfalls
153	- Ignoring reviewer feedback because "it works"
154	- Not running all 12 reviewer perspectives (skipping dimensions)
155	- Treating all findings as equal priority (classify P1/P2/P3 first)
156	- Not creating beads issues for deferred fixes

167	- Not spawning design-craft-reviewer when the Verification Contract includes `design_craft_check`
168	- Skipping design-craft-reviewer for non-UI projects that don't have `design_craft_check` in the contract (correct behavior — not a pitfall)
169	- Not taking visual screenshots when the Verification Contract requires `browser_evidence` or `responsive_check` (reviewers should see what users see)
170	- Taking screenshots on non-UI projects (waste of time — auto-detect first)

185	- security-reviewer P1 findings: all acknowledged or resolved

187	- **Design craft reviewer ran when `design_craft_check` was in the Verification Contract or reported why it was not required**
